text:
couldn't sleep last night. ended up sitting on the kitchen floor at like 2am with the bombilla cup in my hand, the one Mama Lupita gave me back in 2017 with my name engraved on the rim. you know how it goes — the mate had gone cold and I just kept staring at the engraving and thinking about how she died in 2019 and I didn't go see her in her last year. not once. that's the part that won't leave me alone.

I was at Wizeline then, deep in some banking project for a US client, and every time my mom called from Guadalajara saying abuela was asking for me I'd say next month, next month, next quarter. the project's just too hot right now, ma. I had already moved to CDMX by then — well, not quite, that was 2020 in earnest — but I had this whole story I told myself about how I was building a career, how she'd understand, how she always wanted me to do well. she was 87. she had the slow kind of decline where everyone keeps thinking there's time. there wasn't.

the last time I actually saw her in person was Christmas 2018. she made me sit with her in the kitchen in Tlaquepaque while she taught me how to cure the bombilla properly — you fill it with the cold mate first, let it sit overnight, then dump it, then you can drink hot. she said don't use any other cup, this one is yours, and you'll think of me when you drink. I was 25 and I was looking at my phone the whole time. I remember she got annoyed and said déjalo, hijo, the phone will still be there in five minutes. and I put it down for maybe ten minutes and then picked it up again because there was some Slack thing or some bug or some bullshit that did not matter, did not matter, in retrospect did not matter at all.

then 2019 hit and I had every excuse — kept telling myself she's stable, the doctors said stable, my mom said stable, but stable isn't well, stable means she's still there for now, and now is finite. April she was admitted for a UTI that turned septic and within ten days she was gone. I flew in the night before she died. she was already not really there. my mom held my hand and said she's been asking for you for weeks. for weeks. and I couldn't even cry properly because I was so busy being angry at myself that the grief couldn't get through.

today I light the candles for her birthday — which is in three weeks, May 12 — and I do the whole lapsed-Catholic thing of pretending the candles aren't really for her. they are. they're a yearly self-flagellation appointment. Camila keeps the candles for me in a drawer because I'd lose them otherwise. she's good like that. she just had to bury her own mom this year and she still remembers my dead grandmother's birthday, which says something about her and something about the kind of partner she is.

the thing that triggered all this was — I was reading an old WhatsApp thread last night, scrolling way back, and I found voice notes from abuela from 2018. like four of them in a row. she was telling me about a woman in her building who'd had a fall and asking if I was eating enough and complaining about her neighbor's dog. nothing important. nothing urgent. and I had never replied. just left them on read. listened to them, smiled, didn't reply, life kept moving. those are the only voice memos of her voice that I have. four of them. eight minutes total. I have eight minutes of her voice on my phone and I left every single one of them on read.

I listened to the first one again last night. her voice came out of my phone speaker like a ghost — that specific Tlaquepaque-Spanish accent with the Spanish-Spanish 'th' for c-before-i because of the abuelo from Toledo, the one who'd been her grandfather. she said 'mira hijo, te cuento de la doña que se cayó en el ascensor, ya está bien, no te preocupes, pero yo le había dicho hace meses que no se subiera con esos zapatos, ¿te das cuenta?' and the way she said 'te cuento' was the same way she'd said it to me when I was eight, and I realized I had not heard that voice in five years, not once, and I had a recording of it the whole time and never listened.

so why am I telling you this. I think because I've been reading Chiang's Exhalation and there's that thing about how the past is a place you can't return to, only the present where you carry it, and I've been carrying this badly. before that I was on Borges, and there's this Borges line about how a man's whole life can fit inside one moment of remorse. I scoffed at it when I read it. I'm not scoffing tonight.

I want to write to my mom this week. just a long message. tell her I'm sorry I didn't help more during abuela's last year. she'll say it's fine, she'll say abuela understood, she'll say no te mortifiques, hijo, but I want to say it anyway. partly for me, I know. mostly for me probably.

and the kicker — I'm planning a trip to Guadalajara in like six weeks for my brother Mateo's birthday and I keep thinking I should go a day early and visit the cemetery. I haven't been since the funeral. five years and I haven't been. that's the thing about grief I'm only just figuring out — the avoidance compounds. each year you don't go makes the next year harder to go because now you have to explain to yourself why you didn't go for five years instead of four. I just need to break the loop. one bus ride, one bunch of flowers, one hour sitting there. that's the whole task. nobody's asking for more than that. it's not like I have to make a speech or atone publicly. just go, sit, leave. but the not-going has built up into this fortress of dread and I can feel that fortress in my chest right now, the walls of it.

she was — let me write this down because I'm afraid I'll start to forget. she was 4'11. she had hands like sandpaper from forty years of cooking. her laugh sounded like a small bird. she made the best mole in the family, the one with chocolate and three different chiles, and she'd refuse to write down the recipe because, she said, you have to be there to learn it, and now I'll never learn it because I was not there. she told me one time that the only thing she regretted in her life was not learning to read music, because as a girl in Tlaquepaque in the 1940s nobody taught girls music and she would have liked to play the piano. she said this offhand, not bitter, just a fact. she gave me everything. she gave me the bombilla cup. she gave me the way I think about food. she gave me the certainty that you can make beauty from very little if you have patience. and I gave her, in return, eight minutes of voice notes left on read.

there's a thing my mom said at the funeral that I keep coming back to. she said abuela had asked, in her last lucid week, whether I was happy. just that. she didn't ask whether I was successful or whether I was making money or whether I was going to come visit. she asked whether I was happy. and my mom said yes, mom, he's happy, he's working on something he loves. and I want to believe that was true. at the time, working at Wizeline on bank middleware, it was not really true. I was tired and adrift and chasing a number. but my mom said yes for the kindness of letting abuela go thinking the kid she loved was okay. and that is the kind of mother I have. and that is the kind of grandmother I had. and those are not facts I can fix in retrospect — they are facts I can only carry forward and try to deserve.

so the lesson I keep almost learning and not quite learning is that the present-tense version of love is the only one that counts. listening to a voice note when it arrives is love. answering it is love. flying down for one weekend even when the project is hot is love. I had a perfect resume of postponed love by the time she died. the resume did not move the needle. she did not get to consume any of it.

okay sorry. I asked for nothing here, I just needed to put it somewhere that wasn't in my own head at 2am. back to work in a bit. there's a Postgres connection pool issue I should probably look at but honestly nothing on my screen feels real today. when the indices stop mattering for an hour it usually means the heart is doing some other work, and I should let it.

the candle is still lit on the windowsill. I'm going to leave it lit until it's done. I'll write the message to my mom while it burns. that feels like the right shape for the morning. then I'll book the flight. then I'll text my brother and tell him I'm coming a day early because I want to go to the cemetery, would he meet me there. one task at a time. small steps. break the loop.

final note before I close this thread for the morning: I've been thinking about why this particular grief is so heavy this year and not last year. it's been five years. by the actuarial schedule of grief I should be in the 'gentle remembering' phase, not the 'crying-on-the-kitchen-floor-at-2am' phase. and I think the answer is — Camila lost her mom this year. her grief has, in some way, given me permission to access mine. when she's been sad about Beatriz I have sat with it, and sitting with hers has reawakened mine. that's a thing nobody warns you about. shared grief in a relationship is contagious in both directions; it amplifies, it doesn't average out.

but also — and this is the harder reading — I think I had been outsourcing the grief work. I had been letting my mom carry the abuela-related sadness for the family, and I'd assumed that as long as my mom was managing it, I didn't have to. that is a particularly mean kind of avoidance because it relied on my mom's competence at grief to cover for my own incompetence. she has been carrying both — her own mother's death and my unprocessed version of it — for five years. that's not fair. I am taking my piece off her hands now. that's part of what going to the cemetery is for. it's not just for me. it's a way of saying to my mom: I am here, I am also doing this work, you don't have to carry both halves anymore.

okay. enough. the candle is at about half. I will let it finish.

okay back to the pose-est thing. mediapipe is still a revelation after the tensorflow pain. running on CPU and I'm getting like 28-30 fps on Pablo's roundhouse footage which is more than enough for the kind of feedback I want to give. landmarks are smoother across frames than tf was even with the smoothing filter I had hand-rolled.

but I hit a wall on hip rotation. the way mediapipe gives you 33 landmarks, the hip ones are basically just left_hip and right_hip points, which gives me the pelvic angle in 2D but I really want torso-vs-pelvis rotation, which needs the spine. there's no spine landmark — you have to infer it from shoulders + hips midpoint, and that vector is super noisy because the shoulders move independently when you punch.

thought about doing a Kalman filter on the spine vector but feels like overkill. tonight I'll try just a windowed median over 5 frames and see if it's stable enough for the feedback I want to give pablo on his switch kicks. he keeps over-rotating his shoulders before his hips, leaks power. the whole project is basically built around catching that one mistake.

side thought: even if the spine vector is too noisy, I can probably show the relative rotation by tracking the shoulder-line angle independently and the hip-line angle independently and just plotting them as two curves on the same chart. the leak shows up as the shoulder curve leading the hip curve in time. that's a strictly weaker signal than torso-pelvis but it might be enough for the feedback I want.

additional consideration — pablo's been complaining for years that profe esteban can't quite articulate what he sees. profe sees it instantly and gives feedback in shorthand ('atrás, atrás, gira la cadera primero') but pablo, like a lot of engineers, wants the visualization. the project is partly for pablo's specific mind: he learns from data, not from feel. half the kickboxers I know are the opposite — pure feel, no data, would actively reject a chart of their own form. pablo will look at a chart of his own form for an hour. so the audience for this thing is narrower than I'd thought. it's people like pablo. that's like 30% of the gym. but it's all 30% I'd be happy to help.

side note for myself: I should set up a benchmark suite for the inference path. right now I'm eyeballing fps from a single video; I should run it against a fixed set of clips and track latency over commits. with mediapipe holding steady I can start optimizing other parts of the pipeline (pre-processing, smoothing, drawing) and watch the numbers move.

streamlit prototype is on the to-do for tomorrow. I want pablo to be able to upload a video and see the kick-by-kick chart inline without me hand-holding him. that's the difference between 'cool tool I built' and 'tool pablo actually uses.' the moment he can do it himself I have built something. before that it's just a demo I keep running for him.

follow-up brain-dump while it's fresh: I should also experiment with mediapipe's task graph API rather than the legacy solutions API. apparently the new graph API has lower latency on multi-frame inference because it can pipeline the landmark prediction across frames. I haven't read the docs in detail yet, but if it's a clean migration I'd like to do it before pablo brings more people from the gym. last thing I want is to have him pitch the tool to four guys at atlas only for it to be 30% slower than my own demo. dev experience matters even for hobby projects. small clarity-of-tooling debts compound.

okay I'm going to vent for a sec. Camila has roped me into Sunday morning yoga at this studio near Parque México and I cannot stand it. it's not the yoga itself even — I'll concede the hip openers do something for the kickboxing. it's the vibe. it's the slow gong music, it's the instructor whispering 'breathe into the discomfort,' it's the woman next to me who smells like patchouli and audibly chants. I have been telling myself I'll find peace there. I have not found peace there.

Camila says I need something to balance the punching. she's not wrong, my hips are tight, my back has been twingy. but every Sunday at 9am I'm in down dog wishing I were anywhere else, watching the clock through my legs. there's a guy in the back who falls asleep in shavasana every week and snores. I am rooting for that guy. he's living the dream.

I bring it up because she suggested doing TWO sundays a week and I think I made an audible noise. she laughed. but yeah. Sunday yoga is the price of love I guess. resentfully unrolling the mat at 8:45 every week. I keep telling myself I'll grow into it. I think I might just stay on this side of it forever and that's also fine. love is sometimes 75 minutes of pretending to enjoy frankincense.

the other thing — and this is where I sound like an absolute baby — is that the studio is full of architects from her firm and other adjacent creative-class CDMX types. I get there in cargo shorts and a faded atlas-box t-shirt and I look like a manual laborer who wandered in by mistake. one of camila's coworkers — Renata, designs hospital interiors, very good at it — clocked me last week mid-warrior-two and made a polite comment about my form. polite. friendly even. but you could feel the gap of understanding. I'm not a yoga person. I'm a man wearing yoga clothes. there is a difference and the room knows it. so I am, for 75 minutes a week, a tourist. I will continue being a tourist for the foreseeable future, because Camila is happy when I'm there and that turns out to be the actual return on investment of the activity. not the hip openers. the partner happiness. that's a thing I am only starting to learn at 32 — that some practices are good not because of their ostensible benefit but because of who they put you next to.

she also pointed out that her firm runs a weekly architecture-and-yoga thing at this same studio and I should consider going just to network. I told her I would consider it, which is partner-language for 'no.' she laughed because she knew. I have all the social capital I can handle in a week from atlas box and the engineering team and the building neighbors. adding 'creative-class CDMX yoga networking' to that pile would simply destroy me. I am self-aware about my introvert budget at 33 in a way I was not at 28.

the other side of this — I keep saying it but it deserves saying out loud — is that doing things you don't enjoy with someone you love is a different category of activity than doing things you don't enjoy alone. alone, the bad activity is just bad. with someone you love, the bad activity is a kind of small offering, and offerings have a different chemistry. I am 33 and I am only just figuring this out. I have been, my whole life, refusing to do things I don't like, and considering this a kind of integrity. it is, on balance, more like a kind of poverty.

RouteOpt did the thing again — p95 over 600ms on the route-batch endpoint during morning rush. Django ORM is doing something gnarly with the geo joins and I cannot for the life of me get it to use the GIST index without hand-writing raw SQL. screenshot of the slow query log coming, do you want it inline or pasted as a code block?

planner is doing the seq-scan thing again on the geo join. let me grab a clean explain analyze before I paste anything. give me five mins. I am at my desk with cold mate in the cup, dog under the desk, light annoyed.

okay here's the slow query, sanitized:

SELECT "deliveries_route"."id", "deliveries_route"."created_at", ST_AsGeoJSON("deliveries_route"."path"), "deliveries_route"."driver_id", "deliveries_route"."vehicle_id", "deliveries_route"."status" FROM "deliveries_route" INNER JOIN "deliveries_stop" ON ("deliveries_route"."id" = "deliveries_stop"."route_id") WHERE (ST_DWithin("deliveries_stop"."location", ST_GeomFromEWKB('\\x010100002...'::bytea), 1500) AND "deliveries_route"."status" IN ('scheduled', 'in_progress')) ORDER BY "deliveries_route"."created_at" DESC LIMIT 100;

EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows a Seq Scan on deliveries_stop with filter ST_DWithin, cost 89421.30, actual time 612.847ms. the GIST index on deliveries_stop.location exists, pg_indexes confirms it. the planner is choosing seq scan because the cardinality estimate is way off — table has 8.4 million rows.

I tried CLUSTER on the GIST index, no help. tried bumping default_statistics_target to 1000 on that column and ANALYZE'd. no help. the only thing that worked was a literal SET enable_seqscan = off; which is not a thing I'm putting in production.

the Django ORM is also generating an unnecessarily ugly query because the WHERE clause has the ST_DWithin on the joined table, not the FROM table, and it's not pushing the spatial filter down. I think I need to either rewrite this with a Subquery + RawSQL or skip Django entirely for this endpoint and do it with psycopg directly. the abstraction is leaking either way.

the deeper question is whether we should keep patching this or accept that the whole RouteOpt service is the bottleneck of the platform and needs a real rewrite. I've been mumbling about this for weeks. Daniela keeps saying yes-let's-discuss-next-quarter and then there's no quarter. 

for tonight, what do you think — RawSQL with a Subquery, or full psycopg escape hatch, or LATERAL join trick (I haven't tried it yet, just heard about it)? I'm leaning LATERAL because it changes the query shape without abandoning Django, but if you've seen it bite at scale I want to know before I commit to it. also — separate question, do you know if CLUSTER on a GIST index is even meaningful? I tried it because someone on stackoverflow said it would help and I now suspect that was bad advice.

the broader pattern I want to flag: this has been the same conversation about RouteOpt for six months. each time the surface details change but the underlying issue is the same — Django ORM is a bad fit for spatial queries at our scale, the planner is unstable on these workloads, and we are losing tail latency we don't have. I'm gonna stop patching and start writing the rewrite memo. I'll send a draft tomorrow.

for the record I tried explain (analyze, buffers) on the same query and the buffer counts confirmed the seq scan story — shared_blks_read in the millions, shared_blks_hit close behind, which means we're spilling out of the buffer cache and hitting the page cache or worse hitting disk on every call. that's the I/O footprint of a query that's scanning 8.4M rows for every invocation. the GIST index gets used in something like 30% of plans randomly. the rest fall back to seq scan. that is not a thing I can configure my way out of. that is a thing I have to architect my way out of. I am increasingly persuaded the only architecture that fixes this is one without django.

talked to my mom for 40 mins last night. she's still rattled about dad's angina episode last week. statin's been bumped, doctor said it's manageable, but she's been a pediatric nurse for 35 years and even she gets shaky when it's her own husband. she keeps asking him to walk in the morning with her and he keeps finding reasons not to. classic dad. survived a triple bypass and still thinks he's invincible.

she asked when I'm coming home next. I said maybe end of next month. she said you said that last month, mijo. fair. fair point. I hung up and felt like garbage for an hour.

the thing is — and I told you this last night already — I keep doing the same thing I did with abuela. I see it. and I'm still doing it. I told Camila and she just looked at me with that particular silence she does and said, you know what to do, right? and yeah. yeah I do. I'll book the flight today.

bought it. June 14, return 16. just two nights but it'll be something. and I told my mom about going to the cemetery a day early. she went quiet on the phone. then she said okay mijo, eso le va a hacer mucho bien a tu corazón. she said it like a nurse, with that half-clinical half-warm tone she has. she also asked if I want her to come with me to the cemetery. I said no, mom, I think I want to go alone the first time. she said okay, and then I'll make pozole for when you're back. that's how she does grief management. food. she has been managing grief through food my entire life and I have, on balance, benefited.

the pozole thing is a whole production. she's been making her own pozole rojo since I was a kid and she will not, under any circumstances, share the recipe in writing. you have to be in the kitchen with her. she said this exact phrase to me when I was 12 and asked, and the phrasing has not changed: 'mijo, las recetas se aprenden con las manos, no con los ojos.' I will be in the kitchen with her on June 14 watching her toast the chiles guajillo and chiles ancho and chiles cascabel and remembering, slowly, the parts of her cooking that abuela taught her, that I never wrote down because I assumed there was time. I am writing more things down now.

she asked about Camila and I said Camila's good, the dog adoption is happening soon, and the rewrite at work is real now. she gets the dog part. she does not really get the rewrite part. she said 'mientras estés contento mijo, eso es lo que importa, ¿estás contento?' and I said yes mom, more than I have been in a long time. and she said good and changed the subject to my brother who apparently bought a new motorcycle without telling her. typical mateo. classic mom-deflection technique. I love that family.

last thought before I close the laptop: I should write down the questions I want to ask my mom about abuela's last few months. things only she knows. things that don't get into family histories because they're too specific. what abuela's hands looked like in those final weeks. what she ate. what her last lucid sentence was. what music was playing in the room when she died. these are details that, if I don't ask now, will be lost when my mom is gone too. I have not been good at asking these questions. I am going to start. I will keep a list.

windowed median on the spine vector worked. pablo's roundhouse vid is now showing a clean torso-rotation curve. need to figure out the threshold for 'leak' but the signal is there. tomorrow I'll throw it in streamlit and let pablo poke at it.

actually let me think about the threshold more carefully. the 'leak' I'm looking for is when the shoulder-line angle leads the hip-line angle by more than ~80ms in the lead-up to contact. that's the textbook biomechanical fault — power generation should start at the hip and travel up. when it starts at the shoulders, the hip arrives late and the kick has 'no foundation,' as profe puts it.

so the threshold is really a delay-between-curves measurement. cross-correlation should give it to me cleanly. shift one curve against the other in 16ms increments (1 frame at 60fps) and find the lag that maximizes correlation. for a clean kick the lag should be 0 to 50ms (hip leads shoulders by half a frame). for a leaky kick it inverts — shoulders lead hips by 80-150ms.

plotting that as a single number per kick attempt would let me show pablo a per-kick score over a session. that's more valuable than any individual visualization. session-level trends are what people remember and what they can train against.

I'll have a number for him by Friday.

the specific cross-correlation pattern, for future me: I'll downsample both curves to 60Hz, window them around the contact moment (defined as the frame where ankle velocity peaks), and run scipy.signal.correlate over a +-200ms window. argmax of the correlation gives lag in samples; multiply by frame interval. report as 'shoulders lead hips by 47ms (yikes)' or 'shoulders lead hips by 8ms (acceptable)' or 'hips lead shoulders by 12ms (clean kick).'

the contact-moment detection is the hairy part. ankle velocity is noisy because the foot is small and mediapipe can't track it precisely on motion blur. I might use the knee instead — knee extension is smoother and the peak knee-extension frame is a reasonable proxy for contact in roundhouses. won't generalize to teeps or front kicks though. for the V1 demo with pablo I'll just have him do roundhouses and we'll deal with kick-type detection later.

side thought: I should record his sessions over time, not just one-off, so we can show drift. one chart of 'lag over the last 12 sessions' would be more compelling than any single chart. a tiny sqlite db of session results plus a streamlit page that pulls them and trends them. that's the v2.

one final note for the future-me: the sliding-window median approach for the spine vector ended up being shockingly robust against motion blur. mediapipe's per-frame confidence scores correlate with how much I should trust the smoothed signal — when confidence dips below 0.7 I increase the window from 5 to 9 frames and accept the latency cost. the kicker is that pablo can't tell the difference between a 5-frame window and a 9-frame window in the visualization, which means I have a free knob for noise robustness. small wins.

yoga this morning. instructor said something I'm still chewing on — she said the body keeps a more honest record than the mind. I rolled my eyes about three times during class but on the walk home I kept thinking about it. I went into shavasana and the second I closed my eyes I was thinking about abuela again. just there. the body had been holding it. I'd walked into the studio thinking about a Postgres index and walked out crying in the hallway like a clown in lulu pants.

Camila pretended not to notice and bought me a juice. I think she's been waiting for me to fall apart about Mama Lupita for like two years. she's been doing the strategic-not-pushing thing.

the juice place she took me to is this little spot on Sonora, run by a woman who knows Camila by name. Camila ordered for both of us and they made us sit on the bench outside in the sun. she didn't say anything for like ten minutes. she just sat next to me with her shoulder touching mine. that is, I am realizing, the actual yoga. not the down dogs. the silence on a bench afterward, with a partner who knows you well enough to not fill it.

okay I'll stop. yoga was actually fine today. don't tell her I said that. seriously, don't. I have a reputation to protect. I will continue to grumble on the walk to the studio. it's part of my identity now. but I will go. I will keep going.

there's a thing I am only now letting myself see. abuela's death and Camila's mom's death happening in the same five-year window has put a particular kind of weight on this relationship. we have both buried mothers, technically — abuela was my second mother in a way, and Beatriz was Camila's. we have a shared vocabulary for grief that other couples don't have. we know what it feels like to lose the person whose voicemails you never returned. we know that the year-after is harder than the year-of. we know that the scent of someone's perfume in a stranger on the metro can ruin your whole afternoon.

she didn't push me on the cemetery thing. she just bought me a juice. but she has also been sitting with me through the bombilla mornings and the candle nights and she has, without saying it, made the apartment a place where I am allowed to be a mess about my dead grandmother. that's a partner gift I did not know to ask for. I am quietly, internally, embarrassingly, in awe of her competence at this. when I was 25 I would have laughed at the idea that the romantic gift I most wanted was 'someone who lets me grieve loudly without trying to fix it.' but here we are. and I am 33.

the shavasana cry was, in retrospect, the first crack. I have been holding the abuela grief in a kind of structured silence for five years and the silence does not actually structure the grief; it just delays it. the grief was always going to come out, and it picked yoga at parque méxico because that's where my schedule allowed it. the body really does keep the more honest record. yoga instructor 1, my prefrontal cortex 0. I will not be telling her she was right. that's between me and the universe.

tried the LATERAL join thing. it worked, sort of. let me walk through what I did because I want to remember the path even if we end up rewriting this whole service.

original Django ORM-generated query, after I reorganized the WHERE clause:

```
SELECT r.* FROM deliveries_route r WHERE r.status IN ('scheduled', 'in_progress') AND r.created_at >= '2026-04-23' AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM deliveries_stop s WHERE s.route_id = r.id AND ST_DWithin(s.location, ST_GeomFromEWKB('...'::bytea), 1500)) ORDER BY r.created_at DESC LIMIT 100;
```

p95 went from 612ms to 287ms. better. still not great. the EXISTS subquery makes the planner short-circuit nicely once it finds one matching stop per route, which is the pattern we want. but the planner still has the same fundamental problem: cardinality estimates on ST_DWithin are bad, and it's flipping between index scan and seq scan on the deliveries_stop table depending on how many matching stops it thinks the spatial filter will return.

I dug deeper. the actual EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT JSON) for a slow case showed:

```
-> Index Scan using deliveries_route_pkey on deliveries_route r (cost=0.43..89421.30 rows=4287 width=512)
   Filter: (status = ANY ('{scheduled,in_progress}') AND created_at >= '2026-04-23'::timestamptz)
   -> SubPlan 1
     -> Bitmap Heap Scan on deliveries_stop s (cost=4.42..23.18 rows=1 width=0) (actual rows=0.43 loops=4287)
       -> Bitmap Index Scan on deliveries_stop_location_idx (cost=0.00..4.42 rows=8 width=0)
         Recheck Cond: (location && ST_Expand(...))
```

that looks healthy at first glance — the bitmap index scan is using the GIST index. but the loops=4287 is the smoking gun: we're running the bitmap heap scan 4287 times, once per candidate route, and even though each one is cheap (0.43 rows actual on average), 4287 * the heap-fetch cost adds up.

the LATERAL trick I'd heard about goes like this:

```
SELECT r.id, r.created_at, ... FROM deliveries_route r,
  LATERAL (SELECT 1 FROM deliveries_stop s WHERE s.route_id = r.id AND ST_DWithin(s.location, $1, 1500) LIMIT 1) match
WHERE r.status IN (...) AND r.created_at >= $2
ORDER BY r.created_at DESC LIMIT 100;
```

this is roughly equivalent to EXISTS, but with the LATERAL the planner can sometimes choose to flip the join order and start from the spatial side first — i.e. get the matching stops first via a single GIST index scan, then look up their parent routes. on our data shape that flipped path is 5-10x faster because the spatial filter is highly selective: of 8.4M stops, maybe 200 are within 1500m of the query point.

the thing is, the planner WILL NOT reliably pick the flipped order. sometimes it does, sometimes it doesn't. the tipping point is statistics-driven and apparently fragile. I tested it on five different query points and got the flipped plan on three, the original plan on two. the original plan still gave 287ms (matching EXISTS perf). the flipped plan gave 41ms.

I considered using pg_hint_plan to force the join order. that works but it's a foot-gun: hint pinning ages badly because the data shape changes underneath you. I've seen hints that helped at the time of writing but became actively harmful 18 months later when the data had grown.

the other thing I considered was rewriting as two queries: first SELECT route_ids from deliveries_stop with the spatial filter, then SELECT routes WHERE id IN (those_ids) ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 100. that's ugly because it's two roundtrips, but it gives me deterministic plans and the spatial side gets the GIST index every time. I might do this if I can't talk Daniela into the rewrite.

but here's the thing — the fact that this micro-optimization is the headline win of my week is kind of the symptom, right? we are scraping pennies off a Django service that is fundamentally too slow for the throughput we have. the planner is brittle on our hot path and I'm playing fragile games with hints and join orders. every fix is a fix against a specific shape of the data, and the data shape is not under my control. customers add high-density tenants. stops grow. row distributions shift. the planner regression that's lurking in this service is an outage waiting to happen, not a problem already solved.

I'm going to put this whole thing in a doc and bring it up with Daniela on Friday. with metrics. with a recommendation. I have been mumbling about a rewrite for months and mumbling has gotten me exactly nothing. the doc will have three options:
1. status quo + LATERAL hint + load testing — 2 weeks, low risk, low ceiling
2. partial rewrite of the spatial query path in psycopg with explicit query plans — 6 weeks, medium risk, ~5x throughput
3. full rewrite of RouteOpt in a new stack (rust) — 16-20 weeks, higher risk, 10-30x throughput, removes Django ORM as a dependency entirely

I'll lead with option 1 because that's what an honest memo does. but I will spend the most words on option 3 because that's where the actual ROI is. the question is whether Daniela will give me the air to write option 3 without flinching at the word 'rust.' I think she will. I think she's been waiting for someone on the team to write this memo and I'm going to be the one. 

okay. enough thinking. shipping the LATERAL change behind a flag tonight to take the pressure off. memo by end of week.

appending: I am also writing a 'Q&A' section at the end of the memo for objections I expect Daniela to anticipate from leadership. the questions:

- 'why not Go instead of Rust?' — Go would let the team ramp faster (1-2 months vs 3-4 months for rust) and the perf delta is small at our scale. the case for rust over go: type-system guarantees on geometry data (a coordinate that can never be nil at compile time is a class of bug we never have to debug), borrow checker catches lifetime errors that go's GC would mask, and the ecosystem around postgres + spatial data is converging on rust faster than go (sqlx vs go's sql/database). also — and this is more honest than I'd put in the memo — rust's compile-time enforcement is the difference between a service that's 'mostly correct' and a service that's 'provably correct in important ways.' for a service that handles spatial routing for paying customers, I want the latter.

- 'what if the team can't ramp on rust?' — the team has two seniors with rust experience and we'll allow 6 weeks of structured ramp time at the start. if Andrés is not productive in rust by month 3, we slow phase 1 and bring in a contractor for a quarter while he ramps. the ramp risk is real and it's the single biggest thing I lose sleep over. but rust's learning curve is steepest in the first three months and flattens hard after that; we'll know by month 4 if we're in trouble.

- 'what's the cost of running both services in parallel during phase 3?' — roughly 2x infrastructure cost on the affected services for ~3 months. small compared to the cost of an outage. and we get to compare them in production for safety. dual-read alone justifies the cost.

- 'why now and not in q3?' — because the data shape that triggered tonight's planner regression is going to keep getting worse. we onboard 4-6 new tenants a quarter, each adding ~500k stops on average. by q3 we will have an additional 2-3M stops, deeper into the regime where the planner's cardinality estimates are unreliable. the longer we wait, the worse the python service's tail latency will get and the more emergency mitigation work we'll do. starting now means we can do this calmly. starting in q3 means we'd be trying to architect a rewrite while also fighting fires every week.

that's the doc. I will sleep on it tonight and proofread it tomorrow. if it survives my morning self with no big edits I'll send it Monday. one shot at this conversation; I need to make it count.

appendix to my thinking: I went and looked at the postgres mailing list archives this afternoon for similar planner-regression cases on ST_DWithin. it's a known issue. the cardinality estimator for spatial functions is heuristic and has been improved repeatedly but it still falls down on borderline-selective queries against big tables. the maintainers acknowledge this. there are some experimental improvements in postgres 17 (we're on 15) that would help, but upgrading is a six-week project on its own and would not eliminate the issue, just reduce its frequency.

so I had a small clarifying realization: this is not a bug we can fix at the postgres-version level. it's a structural property of the planner's interaction with our data shape. the only way to get reliable plans is to remove the planner's degree of freedom — which means writing the queries in a way that gives the planner only one good option, which usually means manual hint-pinning or the LATERAL trick or just bypassing the ORM entirely. the python service is bad at all three because django's ORM rewrites queries unpredictably. the rust service can be good at all three because sqlx gives us raw SQL with compile-time checks.

I added this as a fourth bullet to the memo: 'we cannot fix this in postgres; we can only fix it by writing queries that constrain the planner.' that lands well as a structural argument because it says the problem is not bad postgres or bad data but a lack of control over the application-level query shape, which is the most fixable thing in our stack. fixable problems are the ones leadership wants to fund.

drove out to Tepoztlán with Camila on Saturday. La Torta still does ok on the curves. ate too many quesadillas, drank one mezcal and was hammered (lightweight life). she fell asleep in the car on the way back. felt good. just felt — good. sometimes we forget to do that and we should not forget.

the mezcal was at this little place on the way up the hill, run by a guy named Don Beto who's apparently been making mezcal in his backyard for forty years. he poured us each a thumb of an espadín that was so smooth I almost cried. Camila bought a bottle to bring home. it cost more than I'm comfortable saying out loud.

the ride back through the curves was a kind of meditative thing. Camila asleep with her face against the passenger window, the late afternoon light orange through the trees, la torta humming through the gears like she's been doing this for thirty years instead of seven. I didn't think about postgres once. I didn't think about RouteOpt once. I thought about absolutely nothing for about forty minutes which is a state I cannot achieve through meditation, only through driving a Honda through the mountains with someone I love asleep in the passenger seat.

we don't do this often enough. that's a thing I want to say out loud here so I can hear myself say it. we don't do this often enough. the rewrite is going to eat me for the next year. the dog is going to eat me for the next year. there will be more 4am incidents. there will be more 11pm rust sessions. and in the middle of all that we have to keep going up to Tepoztlán sometimes. the going-up is the point.

on the road back I was thinking about how few of these days we have. like, on a generous count maybe twelve a year? we live a 90-minute drive from the mountains and we do this maybe once a quarter. so 4-5 trips a year. multiplied by a finite number of years before life gets more complicated by kids or career or aging parents. the actuarial table for Tepoztlán days is grim if you look at it cold. I want to look at it cold for once and adjust the count.

so here's a small commitment I will write down — once a month, no exceptions, we go somewhere outside the CDMX bubble. don't have to be Tepoz. could be Valle de Bravo, Cuetzalan, Pátzcuaro, Real de Catorce, anywhere. could be just Coyoacán if everything is on fire at work. but the thing on the calendar must be 'we go somewhere together.' Camila will laugh at me trying to schedule romance like a sprint plan. let her. she will also be on board because she has been quietly trying to do this for years.

last night Camila said something at dinner that I want to keep — she said 'we're going to be old together one day, you know?' just like that, in the middle of the meal, no warning. and I — I just got quiet for a second and then said 'yeah, I know.' and then we went back to eating tacos at our kitchen counter under the bad fluorescent. but I have been carrying that sentence for two days. she is, in her quiet way, telling me she's already there. she's already chosen this. the question is whether I am also already there. I think I am. I think I have been for a while. I just have a hard time saying it back.

streamlit app for pose-est is up locally. pablo dropped by yesterday after sparring and we played with it for an hour. video upload, see your stance heatmap and a torso-vs-pelvis curve. he found three things wrong with his roundhouse. one of them was lift-off — his back foot lifts off before contact, which we'd been arguing about for months and now I can show him in numbers. he was kind of stunned by his own footage.

he wants to bring two more people from the gym to test. I said sure. but I want to harden the upload flow first because it currently OOMs on anything over 90 seconds. I'm reading frames eagerly into memory like an idiot. need to switch to a generator pattern with ffmpeg piping frames in chunks.

the project feels alive in a way other side projects haven't. probably because there's a real person on the other side who actually wants to use it. that's the secret of side projects nobody tells you — most of them die because there's no audience, just an idea. the audience is the engine. pablo's eagerness is what's pulling me through the boring parts (refactoring the pipeline, writing the streamlit ui, fighting with ffmpeg argument flags).

I realized today that I should also keep a session log per user. like, store every kick analysis pablo runs, build him a personal trend dashboard. session N showed your shoulder lead averaged X ms; session N+1 averaged Y. drift over time. progress tracking. that kind of thing makes a tool sticky. without it pablo runs his videos once and forgets. with it he's checking his own numbers weekly. I'll sketch the schema this week — sqlite locally, just to keep it simple.

an additional motivator: the gym audience is bigger than just pablo if I do this right. I had Edgar try the streamlit app today on a video of his last sparring round and he stared at the chart for ten minutes silently and then said 'yo no sabía que hacía eso.' that's the line I want from every user. I did not know I was doing that. computer vision feedback against a known-but-unstated movement habit. that is the entire product, in one sentence.

for v0.2 I'll add a comparison mode where you can stack two videos and watch their curves at the same time. shadow-boxing yourself. or comparing your kick to your training partner's. or — and this is the one I'm most excited about — comparing your kick from 6 months ago to your kick today. progress visible. pablo would actually weep, I think. he's been kickboxing for nine years and has zero quantitative measurements of his own progression.

the ffmpeg pipe pattern, for memory: subprocess.Popen with stdout=PIPE, then read in fixed-size byte chunks corresponding to one frame's worth of raw RGB data, decode with numpy.frombuffer + reshape, hand to mediapipe, dispose. you never hold more than a handful of frames in memory at a time. the inference becomes proportional to video length in time, not in memory. simple change, big payoff.

the MIT thread keeps surfacing. I keep reading their papers at midnight. I think the right move is to write a small substack post about what I'm doing — a short technical note about cross-correlation lag for kickbox feedback — and see if it gets any uptake. that's a 4-hour weekend task. low effort, real signal. if a researcher pings me, I have a conversation. if nobody pings me, the side project lives on as a side project and I move on. either outcome is fine. the cost of the experiment is small enough that I should run it.

lit a candle for Mama Lupita's birthday last night. it would have been her 92nd. I told you I do this every year and pretend it's for her — well, last night I just sat with the candle and talked out loud to her for like fifteen minutes which is something I have never done before. I don't know what came over me. probably the conversation we had the other night about the voice notes.

I told her about the rust thing — well it's not officially a thing yet, but I keep thinking about how the Python service is collapsing under its own weight and how the right answer is a rewrite. I told her about Camila, about the dog we keep talking about and not adopting, about the fact that I'm 32 and I haven't really felt like I'm building anything that lasts. I told her I miss her. I told her I'm sorry I didn't visit. she did not respond, which, you know — fine, fair.

the thing that surprised me was that talking out loud actually helped. like if you'd asked me yesterday whether monologuing at a candle would help I'd have said no, I'm a software engineer, that's silly. but the thing inside me that has been twisted up since 2019 felt like it loosened slightly. just slightly. enough that I slept till 6 instead of 4.

I made mate this morning in her cup and the engraving caught the kitchen light a certain way and I just stood there with it for a minute. she'd be 92. she'd be telling me I'm too thin and that I need to call my mother more often. she'd be exactly right. 

Camila walked in halfway through and saw me standing there with the cup and didn't say anything. she just put her hand on my back for a moment and then made breakfast. she has, throughout this year of my grief about her mom and now my grief about my grandmother, never once asked me to be okay. she has only made room for me to not be okay. that's a kind of competence I did not know existed in romantic partners. I am the lucky beneficiary of a kind of emotional engineering she does that I do not understand and could not replicate.

later that morning I watched her chop onions for breakfast tacos and I had this small flash where I imagined she was 60 and I was 65 and we were still standing in this same kitchen, a different kitchen but functionally this one, and she was still chopping onions and I was still drinking mate from this same cup, and that future felt — felt close. felt within reach. felt like the ordinary continuation of where we already are. I have been so worried about whether we'll get to that future, the kid timing, the parent illnesses, all of it, that I forgot to notice that we are already most of the way there in the way that matters. we already have the kitchen. we already have the cup. we already have the mornings. the rest is bookkeeping.

I am going to keep a list this year of moments where I felt the future close like that. I have not done this before. I think the practice itself does some work. when you write down a moment of forward-looking peace it gets harder to forget that those moments exist.

later I wrote a long message to my mom. didn't send it. saved it in drafts. I want to read it again tomorrow before I send to make sure it's not maudlin. it's the kind of message that, if I send it badly, makes my mom worry — she'll think something is wrong with me, instead of right with me. I want her to read it and feel proud of me, not concerned about me. there is a craft to writing emotional messages to parents that nobody teaches. I am learning by trial and error and saved drafts.

wrote the memo. four pages. three sections — current state with metrics, three options (incremental, partial rewrite, full rewrite), recommendation (full rewrite, rust). Daniela said let me read it tonight, we'll discuss tomorrow.

I made the case as boring and metric-driven as I could. p95 trajectory, infra cost growth, on-call pages per quarter (we're at 14 last quarter, was 4 the year before), engineer-hours per feature. the trend lines are bad. the trend lines speak for themselves.

I didn't put the word 'rust' in the title because I didn't want her to bounce off it. I put 'A path to sub-50ms p95 routing' and saved the language choice for page 3.

this is the closest I've come to actually advocating for something at a senior level. nervous. but if not now then when.

the structural rule I followed when writing this — and I want to remember it — is that the recommendation comes after the data, not before. I have read too many memos where the author leads with their answer and then back-fills the analysis. those memos are easy to bounce off because the reader can dismiss the answer before they've engaged with the data. when the data comes first the reader has to engage with it and by the time they hit the recommendation they're 70% of the way to it themselves.

that's a kind of writing trick I learned from Daniela actually — she does this in her own memos. she lets the data crush you flat before she tells you what she thinks. by the time she gives the verdict you're nodding along. I am going to try to write like her for the rest of my career.

one other thing I noticed about Daniela's edit pass on my prior memos: she trims adverbs aggressively. 'extremely', 'incredibly', 'really', 'super', 'massively' all get cut. her thinking is that adverbs are a tell that the author doesn't trust the data to land — you reach for an intensifier when the number isn't speaking for itself. when the number speaks, you don't need 'extremely.' you just need the number.

I went back through my own memo and cut twelve adverbs. it reads tighter. it also reads more confident, which is the paradox: cutting the intensifiers makes the writing more intense. that is a counterintuitive thing I will keep in my notes for the rest of my career. the technical writing playbook I am slowly assembling has, on the first page, in caps: KILL THE ADVERBS.

the data-first pattern I've started thinking of as 'the funnel.' you set up a funnel of facts so that by the time the reader hits the bottom they have only one direction to fall. the recommendation at the bottom is the floor of the funnel. if the funnel is wide enough at the top — covers enough alternatives the reader might suggest — they don't reach for any of them, because they've all been considered and dispatched on the way down.

I also want to record a structural choice — I put the rust recommendation in the body of the doc, not in the title or the executive summary. the executive summary lists 'a path to sub-50ms p95' and three options. the body is where rust appears explicitly. this lets a leadership skim catch the goal and the options without bouncing off the technology choice. by the time someone reads the rust paragraph, they're already invested in the goal. the technology choice becomes 'how do we get there' rather than 'what scary new thing is seb proposing.'

Daniela read the memo. said 'this is annoyingly compelling.' that is probably the highest compliment she gives. she wants to talk Friday. I am buzzing. quick caffeine kick at Cardinal café before I do anything stupid like reply-all the engineering channel.

'annoyingly compelling' is, by daniela standards, basically a standing ovation. she does not give compliments easily. I am going to sit on this feeling for ten minutes and then go back to writing the polygon test cases like a normal person. cardinal café americano was correctly diagnosed. wired. focused.

pablo brought two guys from atlas. Edgar (lefty, southpaw stance) and Ivo (low-kick specialist, hates head movement drills). we recorded six sparring rounds on my phone, ran them through the pose-est. Edgar's hip rotation is genuinely beautiful and I wanted to print the chart and frame it. Ivo, on the other hand, has a stance issue I had not anticipated — he leads with his shoulders before he steps, and the model picked it up.

Profe Esteban came over while we were huddled around my laptop in the corner of the gym, looked at the screen for maybe ten seconds, and said 'sí, mira, eso es exactamente lo que le digo desde hace un año.' which felt like a tiny coronation. I'm holding onto that one for a while.

on the walk home I realized I want this to be more than a side project. I want to study this. like, properly. there's a research-shaped thing here about computer vision feedback in physical training and I have never been the kind of person who lets myself say 'I want to do research' out loud, but here we are. I keep thinking about the MIT Media Lab — affective computing group does adjacent stuff. I read three of their papers last weekend. I should not let myself fall down that hole until the rust thing is in motion at work, but the hole is there.

for now: keep iterating with pablo and edgar and ivo, keep the project alive on weekends, write better feedback than the average sports-tech app would. the research-shape thing can wait for a year.

the MIT thing is dangerous because it would change everything. the affective computing group does work that's very close to what I'm doing on the side, but they have multi-million-dollar budgets and full-time PhD students and access to data I will never have access to. as a pragmatist I should let them do the research and learn from their papers. as a person I keep wanting more.

the pragmatic version is: I keep doing the side project, I publish a small paper or a substack post once it's mature, I see if anyone in academia bites. the romantic version is: I apply for a master's, I uproot my life, I move or do long-distance with Camila, I take a 70% pay cut, I become a researcher. the romantic version is appealing precisely because it's romantic, which is the strongest possible signal that it might be the wrong call. I will sit with it for a year before I let myself decide. the rewrite at work needs me sober.

on the walk home through the gym side-streets I bought tacos al pastor at the corner stand and ate them standing at the counter while watching a stray cat cross calle córdoba. CDMX evening. nube was at home with Camila. I will be home in 12 minutes. the gym sweat is starting to set into the t-shirt. it's been a year of small good things and a year of three big bad things and on the balance the small good things are running ahead. that's the only metric that matters at age 33.

the other thing I want to remember about today is profe esteban's specific phrasing — 'eso es exactamente lo que le digo desde hace un año.' he was saying that he had been telling Ivo this for a year and Ivo had not internalized it. the visualization made it land in 30 seconds. that's a real thing about coaching: words can take a year to absorb. an image takes one viewing. for visual learners specifically — and Ivo is one — the visualization isn't a supplement to coaching, it's the primary delivery mechanism.

I am writing this at 4:17am from the office. we just came out of a four-hour Postgres outage on the production RouteOpt cluster. four hours. I have not slept. there's a coffee stain on my keyboard, the floor is littered with empty mate gourds, and Andrés has been on Zoom from his kitchen the whole time. I'm going to dump everything I can remember while it's fresh because if I don't I'll forget half of it and Daniela's going to want a written-up version on her desk by lunch.

timeline. 11:42pm CDMX time, PagerDuty fires — p99 latency on POST /routes spiking, error rate climbing. I get on. by 11:50 I see we're at 100% errors on the route-batch endpoint. driver app is hard-down. dispatch desk in monterrey is calling me directly because they can't assign night-shift routes. the night-shift coordinator, a woman named Ofelia who I've never met but whose voice I now know intimately, was calling me every 12 minutes for status updates. I tried to keep my voice steady. I don't think I succeeded.

first thing I check is db CPU. 100%. of course. iostat shows disk saturated. pg_stat_activity shows like 380 active connections, all backed up on the same query — the geo-join thing I was complaining about last week. but normally we have 80 conns at peak. why 380? because the connection pool retry logic on the worker side, which I'd noticed was sketchy six months ago and never fixed, was issuing new conns on every timeout, creating a death spiral. we are eating ourselves.

pasted the error from the worker logs because it's gonna haunt me:

```
[2026-04-23 23:47:14 UTC] [worker-23] sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError: (psycopg2.OperationalError) FATAL:  remaining connection slots are reserved for non-replication superuser connections
[SQL: SELECT deliveries_route.id, deliveries_route.created_at, ST_AsGeoJSON(deliveries_route.path), ...]
[parameters: {...}]
[2026-04-23 23:47:14 UTC] [worker-23] worker_pool.retry: opening fresh connection (attempt 4/inf)
[2026-04-23 23:47:14 UTC] [worker-23] sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError: (psycopg2.OperationalError) FATAL:  remaining connection slots are reserved for non-replication superuser connections
[2026-04-23 23:47:15 UTC] [worker-23] worker_pool.retry: opening fresh connection (attempt 5/inf)
[2026-04-23 23:47:15 UTC] [worker-23] sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError: (psycopg2.OperationalError) FATAL:  remaining connection slots are reserved for non-replication superuser connections
```

that 'attempt 4/inf' is the smoking gun. there's no upper bound on retries. it just keeps trying. forever. and each new attempt opens a new conn. the worker pool config has `retry_attempts=None` which sqlalchemy interprets as 'never give up,' and there's no exponential backoff between attempts so each worker is hammering the database with several reconnect attempts per second. multiply by 60 worker pods, each with 8 workers each, and you have ~480 processes simultaneously trying to open new connections every second. the database can't even fail them fast enough; the FATAL responses are themselves consuming I/O.

first attempt at mitigation, around 12:20am: kill the long-running queries and let the pool drain. ran SELECT pg_terminate_backend on like 200 PIDs. it worked for about 90 seconds. then they all came right back because the worker pods kept retrying the same poisoned queries. I should have started by stopping the workers, not by killing the queries. live and learn.

second attempt, 12:55am: scaled the worker fleet down to 1/3. that helped reduce inbound pressure. but now we had a new problem — the autovacuum on deliveries_stop, which had been kicked off mid-incident because of all the dead tuples from the connection churn, was holding a lock that blocked everything else. so we paused autovacuum manually (yes, I know, this is bad practice, do not @ me).

third attempt and the actual root cause hunt: I dug into pg_stat_statements and saw the spatial query I'd been complaining about for weeks. ST_DWithin on a 8.4M-row table. tonight there were two new tenants who'd onboarded with double the route density of our average customer. the planner had been picking seq scan for the borderline-cardinality cases all month, but tonight tipped over. once that flipped, the whole pool clogged.

what I saw in pg_stat_statements (sanitized):

```
 queryid: 8273645... | calls: 19,847 | mean_exec_time: 4127.3 ms | rows: 0 | shared_blks_hit: 4291847
 SELECT r.id, r.driver_id, r.status, ST_AsGeoJSON(r.path)
 FROM deliveries_route r INNER JOIN deliveries_stop s ON s.route_id = r.id
 WHERE ST_DWithin(s.location, $1, $2) ...
```

4127ms mean execution time. that is — that is just a different number than this query had ever shown before. weeks ago it was 280-600ms. tonight it was four full seconds because the planner had picked seq scan and the table was scanning eight million rows for every call. each seq scan was reading shared_blks_hit ~217 buffers per call which on its own isn't catastrophic, but multiplied by 19,847 calls in the time window the I/O was crushing.

fix at 1:50am: I dropped in a literal SET enable_seqscan = off ; for the stuck session and forced index usage. also temporarily added a hint via pg_hint_plan in the prepared statements path. queries finished. errors cleared. workers caught up by 3:40am. we declared all-clear at 4:02am.

four hours of customer-facing downtime. drivers couldn't get assigned. dispatchers were on the phone with customers manually rerouting. monterrey ops manager called me twice saying 'qué onda con esto' and I had nothing for her. our SLA was breached on the contract with our biggest tenant, Logistica Norte, and the support team is going to be on their hands and knees with that account tomorrow. I am dreading the email I will get from their procurement office.

I am going home to sleep for like three hours and then I have to write the post-mortem. Daniela is letting me keep the doc — she's giving me the space to own it because, in her words, 'your memo two weeks ago basically predicted this.' which is true and which is also not making me feel any better, because I'd flagged it and we hadn't moved fast enough.

the gut-punch part of the night was at 2:15am when I realized this was going to be the catalyst that finally forces the rewrite conversation. you should never want production pain to win an argument for you. I am sitting here right now feeling tactical relief and ethical dread at the same time. I'd rather have not had this argument at all than win it this way. but here we are. four hours, 38,000 delayed deliveries, one ops coordinator named Ofelia who I will never meet who probably had the worst night of her career on my watch.

Andrés said something around 3:30am that stuck with me. he said 'esto es lo que pasa cuando avisas y no te hacen caso, jefe.' jefe. he's never called me jefe before. I'm not his jefe. but it sat in my chest because there's a small chance — a small but nonzero chance — that this outage just changed the shape of my career at this company. you don't get to predict an outage and then own the post-mortem and then own the rewrite without something quietly shifting in how people see you.

okay sleep. more later. I missed kickboxing tonight too — Profe is going to read me when I show up Friday looking like a corpse. and the worst part — Camila has been on the couch awake the whole time waiting for me to come home. I texted her from my desk at 2am and she said 'no te preocupes mi vida, take your time, le di de cenar al gato del vecino.' she is the kind of partner who feeds the neighbor's cat at 2am while waiting for her boyfriend to come home from a postgres meltdown. I am extremely lucky and extremely tired.

oh and one more thing before I close the laptop: my hands are shaking. not from caffeine — I drank mate, not coffee, mate doesn't shake you the way coffee does. I think it's adrenaline. I have not felt this kind of full-body shake from work in years. I'd forgotten what acute professional fear actually feels like in the body. it lives in the forearms and the back of the neck. I'd describe it but I'm too tired. just noting it for the record. the body keeps the record.

one more layer I want to capture before I sleep — the human cost during the incident, in the kind of detail I usually omit. Andrés had been on Zoom from his kitchen since 11:50pm. his partner texted me at 4am saying 'is he done?' which is its own kind of incident report. we ate cold leftover takeout I'd brought to the office at midnight. I was wearing yesterday's t-shirt because I had not had time to change. my hands shook from adrenaline (not caffeine — I'd been on mate, mate doesn't shake you) for about two hours after the incident closed. I had a bruise on my elbow from leaning on the desk for so long without realizing it.

# Postgres Production Outage — Month 5 / 2026-04-23

## What
Four-hour customer-facing outage of the RouteOpt service. Route-batch endpoint returned 5xx errors continuously between 23:42 CDMX and 04:02 next-day. Driver assignment was effectively offline for ops centers in Monterrey, Querétaro and CDMX-Norte. Dispatch fell back to manual reassignment.

## When
- 23:42:11 — first PagerDuty alert on /routes/batch p99 > 2s.
- 23:46 — first responder (Seb) on call.
- 23:51 — error rate at 100%.
- 00:03 — incident declared, comms channel opened.
- 00:20 — first mitigation attempt (terminate stuck backends). Partial, brief.
- 00:55 — second mitigation (scale workers down to 1/3, pause autovacuum).
- 01:30 — root cause hypothesis: planner regression on spatial query.
- 01:50 — root cause confirmed; forced index scan, hint applied.
- 02:30 — error rate < 5%.
- 03:40 — workers caught up on backlog.
- 04:02 — all-clear.

Total duration 4h20m, customer-impact duration 3h58m.

## Who
Affected: ~1,400 active drivers across 3 ops centers. 38,000 in-flight delivery slots delayed avg 47 minutes. Three SLA breaches with named tenants (Logistica Norte, FrescaCorp, MoveYa).
Responders: Seb (lead), Andrés (RouteOpt), Lucía from infra, Mateo from data-platform on standby. Daniela on as exec stakeholder from 00:30.

## Where
Primary RouteOpt Postgres cluster, eu-west prod. PostGIS extension on the spatial schema. Specifically: deliveries_stop table, ST_DWithin spatial filter on the route-batch endpoint.

## Why (root cause)
Query planner cardinality misestimate for ST_DWithin on the deliveries_stop table tipped over from index-scan to seq-scan plan once two new high-density tenants brought row volume above 8.4M. Seq scan with a spatial filter on every row at production traffic saturated CPU in 6-8 minutes. Connection-pool retry logic on the worker side then created a runaway storm: poisoned queries re-issued on new connections, exhausting pg_max_connections and locking the cluster.

In one sentence: a planner heuristic flipped, and our retry logic turned a slow query into a self-amplifying outage.

The true root cause is the unbounded retry. The trigger was the planner regression. We must fix the retry — the planner regression is a symptom we can prevent with monitoring but cannot guarantee will never recur.

## Contributing factors (no-blame list)
1. Spatial query was on a known watch-list. A memo flagging RouteOpt performance was circulated 2 weeks prior; rewrite conversation was scheduled but not initiated.
2. Connection-pool retry logic at the worker level had no circuit breaker. This was identified informally 6 months ago in code review but not ticketed.
3. Onboarding of two new high-density tenants happened 9 days ago. No load test was performed to project planner behavior against new row distributions.
4. Autovacuum threshold on deliveries_stop was at default; the connection churn during the incident generated dead tuples that triggered AV mid-incident, causing additional contention.
5. Runbook for 'planner regression on spatial queries' did not exist.
6. Monitoring did not have a per-query p95 dashboard at the planner level — would have surfaced the regression earlier.
7. The runbook for paging order assumed a single-pager primary; tonight we needed three responders on within 15 minutes and the secondary chain was not tested.

## Action items
- (P0, owner Seb, 1 week) Add circuit breaker to worker-side DB retries. Bound at 3 attempts with exponential backoff.
- (P0, owner Lucía, 2 weeks) Add per-query p95 dashboard and alerts on plan-stability metrics from pg_stat_statements.
- (P0, owner Lucía, 1 week) Test the secondary pager chain, document escalation order.
- (P1, owner Seb, 4 weeks) Begin RouteOpt rewrite scoping. (Daniela has approved.)
- (P1, owner Andrés, 2 weeks) Tune autovacuum thresholds on deliveries_stop and deliveries_route.
- (P2, owner team, 6 weeks) Build a load-test harness that runs against synthetic high-density tenants pre-onboarding.
- (P2, owner Seb, 8 weeks) Document spatial-query planner gotchas in a 'PostGIS in prod' runbook.
- (P2, owner customer success, 4 weeks) SLA review with affected tenants and credit issuance.

## What went well
- Pager response: first responder on within 4 minutes of alert.
- Mitigation steps were executed in a sound order — least invasive first.
- Communication channels were clear; no panicked deploys or untested commands.
- Daniela took stakeholder load off the responders so we could stay focused on the system.
- Camila brought tacos to the office at 1am, an act of love and good operational hygiene.

## Lessons (no-blame)
- Slow problems that you've flagged and not fixed are rarely slow forever. The flag is the loud version of a clock ticking.
- Retry logic without circuit breakers is a way of converting transient failures into permanent ones.
- When the planner is part of your contract with users, treat plan stability as a first-class production property — monitor it, alert on it, test for regressions.
- An architecture that requires the planner to behave optimally on a single hot-path query is brittle. The right fix is to remove the dependency on planner behavior, which usually means rewriting the data access layer with explicit query plans or hint-pinning. Long-term: rewrite the service.
- The most important meta-lesson: the prediction-to-action gap was the actual failure. A senior engineer wrote a memo two weeks ago that described, in detail, the failure mode that occurred. The org did not act on the memo with the urgency the data warranted. The prevention of this incident was on the table and we declined to prevent it. Future memos that include a specific predicted failure mode should trigger a defined response within a defined window.

## Sign-off
Draft by Seb. Review requested from Daniela, Lucía, the on-call rotation, and the support team lead. Will be presented at engineering all-hands next Thursday and added to the public engineering wiki.

okay. stack. been chewing on this for two weeks. Rust + Axum + Tokio + sqlx, with Postgres as the storage layer (we're keeping the spatial tables, just rewriting the service). Redis for hot caching of route-fingerprints. that's the bones.

why rust:
- p95 latency target is sub-50ms for route-batch. python+django won't get there without a heroic rewrite that ends up being 70% of a rewrite anyway. might as well rewrite for real.
- memory predictability matters. we run on tight ECS task budgets and the python service has been OOM-ing under load roughly weekly. tokio + sqlx gives us back tail latency and memory.
- the team has zero rust in production but two of us (me, Carla) have shipped rust services before in side projects. Andrés is a strong python person but learns fast.
- the alternative was Go. I went back and forth for three days. Go would be faster to ramp the team. Rust gives us the type-system guarantees that I want for a system where geometry coordinates can never be nil. and frankly — and I won't put this in the doc — I want to learn rust deeply and this is the chance to do it on the company's dime. I'll be honest with the team about that motivation, but it didn't tip the decision; the perf numbers did.

why axum specifically over actix or warp:
- tower middleware ecosystem is the cleanest right now
- axum's extractor pattern is the closest to django's view/serializer mental model, easier ramp for the team
- maintained by the tokio team, so the integration story is tight
- actix had the maintainer drama a few years back and even though it's recovered, my preference is on the more-mainstream maintainer ecosystem
- warp is functional-pretty but our team is more comfortable with the explicit-handler style axum offers

why sqlx over diesel:
- compile-time checked queries against actual schema is the killer feature
- async-first, plays nice with tokio
- diesel's macro-heavy DSL was beautiful but our query patterns are not ORM-friendly (lots of spatial queries, lots of CTEs); we'd be fighting it
- the query!() and query_as!() macros let us write SQL as SQL, which is a relief after years of Django ORM contortions

the one thing I am genuinely worried about: PostGIS support in tokio-postgres / sqlx is not great. there's no built-in encoder for the geography type. I think we'll have to write a custom encoder/decoder and that's a chunk of work nobody on the team has done before. phasing:
- phase 0 (this month): scaffolding, CI, deployment story. just get a hello-world rust service running in our infra.
- phase 1 (months 6-9): port the read endpoints (route-batch, driver-assignment-lookup). these are the ones that hurt.
- phase 2 (months 9-12): write paths and the tricky route-optimizer kernel. hardest part.
- phase 3 (months 12-16): cutover behind a feature flag, dual-write, then cut.
- phase 4 (months 16+): kill the python service.

the risk that's been keeping me up: phase 2. the route optimizer is a TSP-style solver that the original team (none of whom are still here) wrote in pure python with custom heuristics. there are zero tests. there is no spec. it is an oracle: it produces routes that drivers find acceptable, and we know they're acceptable because complaints are rare, and that is the entirety of our validation. porting that to rust is going to require either rewriting the heuristics from scratch (terrifying) or wrapping the existing python code via some kind of interop (slow, but at least correct). one more thing I want to write down because the conversation with daniela today felt like a hinge point — she said something near the end of the meeting that I want to remember. she said: 'you're going to have to learn to be wrong in public for the next 18 months. that's the job.' she said this matter-of-factly, not as a warning. she meant: as tech lead, every architectural choice you make is a public commitment, and some of them will be wrong, and being wrong in public is the cost of the role. you don't get to hide behind 'well I was just one engineer on the team.' you are the one with the title at the top of the doc. you own the wrongness as much as the rightness.

that sat in my chest for a while. I have spent a career being conservatively right — not making bold calls, not committing to the load-bearing architectural decisions, leaving room to retreat. that strategy works fine for an IC. it does not work for a tech lead. tech lead requires committing to a direction with incomplete information and being willing to course-correct in public when new information arrives. course correction in public looks, from the outside, like being wrong. it is, internally, the system working correctly — you committed, you learned, you adjusted. but the public-facing optics are sometimes 'this person was wrong.' kickoff email sent. I read it 14 times. Andrés replied with a fire emoji within 90 seconds. Carla replied with a thumbs up and 'finally.' that's the team. let's go.

the email itself was 600 words and I wanted it to be 300 but I couldn't bring myself to cut anything. it had: the goal in one sentence (sub-50ms p95 by end of year). the stack in one paragraph. the phasing in five bullets. the team in three lines. the risks I'm worried about in five bullets. the cadence (weekly tech-lead syncs, monthly all-hands updates, async standups). the ask of each person in two lines.

the ask of Andrés was to ramp on rust over the next 4 weeks while we scaffold — rustlings + the rust book + read the axum repo. the ask of Carla was to review architecture docs and own the postgis encoder design with me. the ask of Daniela was to advocate for us in budget conversations and shield us from cross-team politics. the ask of myself was to actually write code in the first month rather than disappearing into doc-writing — tech leads who only write docs are a known failure mode and I want to not be one.

the 14 reads were partly anxiety and partly real. on read 7 I caught a sentence that sounded over-promising ('we will hit sub-50ms p95'). changed to 'our target is sub-50ms p95.' targets and commitments are different things and the difference matters a lot in writing. let's go.

the email pattern I want to remember for next time, because I will write more of these as a tech lead: lead with the goal, follow with the stack-and-phasing, end with the personal asks. don't put risks first or you'll demoralize the team before they finish reading. don't put risks last or it looks like you're hiding them. put them in the middle, as the natural midpoint between the optimism of the goal and the agency of the asks.

also: signed off with 'looking forward to building this with you' rather than 'thanks' or 'cheers.' that is a small phrasing thing I learned from Daniela. closings set the tone of the next conversation. 'thanks' is closing a transaction. 'looking forward' is opening a collaboration. tech-lead voice is, increasingly, just Daniela's voice running through my fingers.

the clearest tonal lesson from this email: be specific about what each person is being asked to do, and trust them to do it. vague asks ('please support the project') are demoralizing because they imply distrust. specific asks ('please review the architecture doc by friday and flag concerns about the postgis layer specifically') are energizing because they communicate that you've thought about the person's role and respect their time. I learned this from Daniela. I will continue learning it from Daniela for as long as she's around.

one more pattern I caught while drafting: the tone of the kickoff has to be honest about the hard parts but not despairing about them. specifically, the postgis blocker had to be in the email — I owed the team an honest accounting — but it had to land as a known challenge with a credible plan rather than as a reason to doubt the project. I phrased it as 'we will write a custom encoder; carla and I have scoped this; expect 1-2 weeks of focused work in phase 0.' that sentence does the work of acknowledging the risk and committing to a mitigation in the same breath. that's the kind of writing I want to keep doing.
