repo: brujula-routeopt-rs, cargo workspace with three crates:
- routeopt-core (domain types, Route, Stop, Driver, Vehicle, geometry types)
- routeopt-db (sqlx wiring, migrations, query helpers)
- routeopt-api (axum app, handlers, middleware)

crate layout because monolith with internal boundaries is way easier to refactor later than a premature multi-service split. carla pushed for this and she's right. she pointed out that micro-service boundaries are the wrong question early — the right question is module boundaries within a binary, and if we get those right we can split out later if we ever need to. micro-service-first architectures tend to fossilize wrong boundaries because the cost of moving code between services is high. monolith-first lets the boundaries find themselves.

cargo.toml relevant pins:
```

resolver = "2"
members = ["routeopt-core", "routeopt-db", "routeopt-api"]

[workspace.dependencies]
```

decisions inside that file:
- resolver = "2": absolutely required for workspace feature unification, especially with tokio/sqlx interactions
- tokio with full features: yes, we'll use it all (rt-multi-thread, macros, signal, sync, etc). don't waste a week chasing a minimal feature set that breaks under composition
- sqlx 0.7: latest stable. 0.8 was just released but I want a few months of bake time before we depend on it
- runtime-tokio-rustls vs runtime-tokio-native-tls: rustls because we want a single TLS implementation across the team, no system-openssl gotchas in CI
- thiserror for library errors, anyhow for application errors. standard pattern.
- uuid v7 because we want time-ordered ids for our routes. v4 is random and v1 leaks mac addresses. v7 is the right answer for a young codebase.

crucially missing: anything geo-related beyond the bare geo crate. that's the postgis blocker I was worried about. more on that in a sec.

handler shape — sketch of what a port of /routes/batch looks like:

```
async fn route_batch(
    State(db): State<DbPool>,
    Query(q): Query<RouteBatchQuery>,
) -> Result<Json<Vec<RouteSummary>>, AppError> {
    let rows = sqlx::query_as!(
        RouteRow,
        r#"
        SELECT r.id, r.created_at, r.driver_id, r.status as "status: RouteStatus"
        FROM deliveries_route r
        WHERE r.status = ANY($1::route_status[]) AND r.created_at >= $2
        ORDER BY r.created_at DESC LIMIT $3
        "#,
        &q.statuses as &[RouteStatus],
        q.since,
        q.limit as i64
    )
    .fetch_all(&db).await?;
    Ok(Json(rows.into_iter().map(Into::into).collect()))
}
```

that's the easy version without spatial filters. compile-time-checked query. it's gorgeous. I cannot stress enough how nice sqlx is when the schema is real.

the spatial version is the hard one. ST_DWithin in a sqlx query right now would require me to bind a Geography parameter and tokio-postgres doesn't know how to serialize that. I've been digging into how rust-postgis tried to solve it (it's old, doesn't really work with sqlx) and I think I'm going to end up writing a small encoder crate that converts our internal Geography type to EWKB on the wire, plus a decoder for results. I'll talk about that when I dig into it.

middleware stack so far: trace_layer with structured json logs, request_id middleware (uuid v7 again, time-ordered for trace correlation), timeout layer at 5s, compression layer. nothing fancy. each piece is a tower::Layer that wraps the inner service.

deployment: we're using ECS Fargate at brújula. I had the rust binary running on a dev task by day 3. binary size is 18MB, startup time is ~120ms (vs the python service's 6 seconds). that alone is going to halve our deploy times. dockerfile is debian:bookworm-slim base (~30MB total image after layers), with the rust binary copied from a separate builder stage. CI is GitHub Actions, build takes 4m20s on a cold cache and 90s on a warm cache.

CI pipeline shape:
- cargo fmt --check (10s)
- cargo clippy -- -D warnings (90s cold, 30s warm)
- cargo test --workspace (60s)
- cargo build --release (4m cold, 60s warm)
- docker build (90s)
- docker push to ECR (30s)
- ECS task-definition update (5s)
- service deploy (90s rolling)

total: 8 minutes cold, 5 minutes warm. python service was 12 minutes cold mostly because of pip install and the docker image being 800MB. we have already saved meaningful CI time and we haven't shipped a feature.

things on the to-do list for phase 0 closing:
- secrets management story (we use AWS Secrets Manager; need to pull DB password at startup not at build)
- structured config story (figment crate with toml + env override)
- migration runner that runs sqlx migrations on startup but doesn't block traffic if we're past the latest version

phase 0 deliverable target: hello-world rust service receiving traffic in dev environment by end of month. on track. probably 2-3 days ahead actually because the deployment story came together cleanly. carla and I are pair-debugging the secrets-manager fetch tomorrow morning because she's seen this exact problem before in a previous gig and I have not.

additional architecture decisions I want on the record while they're fresh:

logging: I went with tracing + tracing-subscriber emitting JSON to stdout. ECS captures stdout and forwards to cloudwatch, then we ingest into datadog via lambda. the JSON shape is: {timestamp, level, target, message, span_id, trace_id, fields...}. structured logs are non-negotiable for any production service in 2026; the days of grep-in-anger on text logs are over.

configuration: figment with toml + env override. our env in ECS is a known set of variables; figment lets us layer config sources cleanly. I considered config-rs and the 12-factor pure-env approach; figment is the cleanest of the three.

migrations: sqlx migrate. migrations live in /migrations as numbered .sql files. on startup we run sqlx::migrate!() against the pool. for prod we use a separate migration runner (a little CLI binary in the same workspace) so we can run migrations as a deploy step before the new code rolls out. running migrations on app-startup is a footgun in multi-replica deployments — you can have multiple replicas trying to migrate simultaneously. we lock with a postgres advisory lock to prevent that.

feature flags: we use a small wrapper around launchdarkly. the rust SDK is decent. flags are evaluated at request-time, not at startup. flag changes propagate within ~30s. the cost is one network call per flag eval which is negligible (cached locally with TTL).

caching: redis for hot caches (route-fingerprints, driver-status). single-redis-cluster setup, deadpool-redis for the connection pool. cache TTL: 60s for fingerprints, 5s for driver-status (driver positions move fast). cache-aside pattern, not write-through; the write path stays simple.

error handling: thiserror for library/domain errors with explicit variants, anyhow for application/handler errors with chain context. errors carry a stable string code for client consumption (e.g. 'ROUTE_NOT_FOUND', 'INVALID_GEOMETRY'). the HTTP layer maps error variants to status codes via an IntoResponse impl on AppError.

graceful shutdown: tokio::signal listener catches SIGTERM (sent by ECS on task drain), drains the connection pool, completes in-flight requests with a 30s timeout, then exits. critical because ECS rolling deploys depend on a clean shutdown signal. python service had this wrong for two years and we'd see occasional 5xx during deploys until we fixed it; rust gets it right out of the gate.

that's the shape. I will memorialize this in an architecture doc next week. for now, scaffolding is done, hello-world is up, and we're ready to start porting actual endpoints. carla and I are pair-debugging the postgis encoder OID resolution tomorrow morning. coffee or mate, her choice.

one additional thing I want to capture — the 'monolith with internal boundaries' decision is something I want to come back to in a year and grade. the alternative path was 'three microservices from day one' and several engineers from a previous team whispered that we'd regret monolithing. I considered the case carefully and chose monolith because (a) we are 3 engineers, microservices for a 3-engineer team is operational pain we don't need, (b) we don't yet know where the right boundaries are, and (c) we can split later if we need to.

the boundary-discovery argument is the killer one. when you split prematurely, you cement boundaries that are wrong, and the cost of moving code between services is much higher than the cost of moving code between modules in the same binary. monolith-first lets us learn the boundaries empirically — we'll see which modules touch each other, which need their own deploy cadence, which would benefit from independent scaling — and split based on data rather than guesses. carla agrees. andrés is neutral but trusts us. daniela is on board.

that said, the failure mode I am keeping an eye on is the 'distributed monolith' — a system that's monolithic in deploy but split in code in ways that make it impossible to refactor. the way to avoid this is to be ruthless about module boundaries: if module A imports module B and module B imports module A, we have a cycle and we have to fix it before merge. cargo's compile-time module-cycle prevention helps with this — the rust compiler refuses to compile circular module dependencies, full stop. that's a free guardrail and I am happy to lean on it.

okay so the postgis blocker. I've been heads down for two days on this and I want to think out loud because the more I dig the more it looks like a multi-week problem rather than a multi-day one, and I want to convince myself I'm not over-engineering it before I commit.

the problem: tokio-postgres (which sqlx uses under the hood for the postgres driver) does not natively support the postgis Geography type. you can use the bytea fallback with ST_AsEWKB / ST_GeomFromEWKB on the SQL side and pass raw bytes through the wire, but then you lose the type-safety advantage of sqlx::query_as! macros — the macros want a rust type that implements Type<Postgres> + Decode + Encode, and Geography ain't it out of the box.

what I tried first, day 1: just passing geometry as bytea everywhere. works but ugly. all my handlers end up doing manual ST_GeomFromEWKB($1::bytea) inside the SQL string. it works, the queries run, but I lose the compile-time type checking on geometry parameters and I have to remember to wrap every input. that's exactly the kind of leak that causes prod outages later. doable but not what I want for a service we're going to be living with for years.

what I tried day 2: there's a community crate called geozero that has some postgis support, in particular geozero::wkb::Encode/Decode wrappers. plugged it in. it works for the simpler Geometry type but the Geography type has subtle differences — geography is always WGS84 spheroidal, geometry is planar — and the OID for the type is different in the postgres catalog. tokio-postgres figures out OIDs at connect time, and our schema uses geography (which is the right call for a global last-mile delivery system), and the OID isn't in tokio-postgres' default known-types list.

I dug into the tokio-postgres source to confirm. the Type::other() escape hatch lets you supply a custom OID at runtime, but it's awkward — you have to thread the OID through your application state, and the sqlx macro layer doesn't expose a clean way to use Type::other() with the compile-time-checked queries we want to use everywhere.

the cleanest path I can see: write a custom encoder for our internal Geography type that does:
1. implements sqlx::Type<Postgres> with type_info reading the actual OID at runtime from a once-cell that we initialize at pool startup with a SELECT oid FROM pg_type WHERE typname = 'geography'
2. implements sqlx::Encode<'_, Postgres> by serializing to EWKB bytes — header (endianness flag, type, srid), then coordinates
3. implements sqlx::Decode<'_, Postgres> by reading EWKB from the bytes the server sends

step 2 is the chunk. EWKB is well-documented but I've never written a binary encoder in rust. I'll lean on the geozero crate for the actual byte layout reference but write my own type wrapper that the sqlx macros can use.

EWKB byte layout, in case future me needs the cheat sheet:
- byte 0: endianness flag (0x00 big, 0x01 little)
- bytes 1-4: type integer (lower bits = geometry type, upper bits = flag bits for SRID present, Z, M)
- bytes 5-8: SRID (if SRID-bit set) — for geography this is always 4326
- bytes 9+: coordinate data, format depends on geometry type

for a Point: just 16 bytes of two f64 (x, y), little-endian per the flag byte
for a LineString: u32 num_points, then num_points * 16 bytes of (x, y) f64s
for a Polygon: u32 num_rings, then for each ring: u32 num_points, then num_points * 16 bytes
for a MultiPolygon: u32 num_polygons, then for each polygon: full nested polygon EWKB (yes including its own header byte) — this is the tricky one because each sub-polygon has its own endianness flag and type integer

the nested-headers thing in MultiPolygon is the part that confused me for a full afternoon. WKB and EWKB are spec'd that way — each sub-component is independently parseable. I had been writing my multipolygon encoder as one big flat byte sequence and the postgres server kept rejecting it with 'invalid input syntax for type geography' which is the most useless error message in the database world. once I understood that each sub-polygon needed its own (endianness flag + type code) prefix, the encoder started working.

things I considered and rejected:

A) text-format binding. you can tell tokio-postgres to use the text protocol instead of binary, in which case you'd encode geographies as the EWKT string ('SRID=4326;POINT(-99.13 19.43)'). this works but it's slow (text parsing on the server side) and ugly (you're doing string concatenation in your bindings). it also still requires the OID dance for the type info. nope.

B) GeoJSON binding via jsonb. encode geographies as GeoJSON, bind as jsonb, then ST_GeomFromGeoJSON in the query. this is clean but adds two json conversions per query and the GeoJSON support in PostGIS is slower than EWKB by 3-5x in my benchmarks. nope.

C) generate code via build.rs. write a build script that runs against the schema and generates the type wrappers automatically. this is what diesel does. I considered it for 20 minutes and decided no — we have one custom type (Geography) and not a forest of them, so the boilerplate-saving doesn't justify the build-time complexity.

what I am going to NOT do: try to upstream this into tokio-postgres or sqlx. that's a 6-month conversation with maintainers and we don't have the time. I'll keep it as a brujula-internal crate (routeopt-postgis or similar) and we can open-source it later if it works out.

estimate: 1 week of work. 2 if I hit weird edge cases with mixed Geometry/Geography in the same query (we have a few tables that mix). I'm going to scope it narrowly to start — just Point and LineString geographies, which covers 95% of our schema. polygons can come later.

worried about: production wire-format compatibility. EWKB has a bunch of optional flags (Z, M, SRID present/absent) and if we get one wrong we'll send malformed bytes that postgres rejects, and the error messages are awful (just 'invalid input syntax for type geography'). going to write hex-dump tests against known EWKB samples from psql before I trust anything. the test setup: emit a known geography from psql via SELECT ST_AsEWKB(ST_GeographyFromText('SRID=4326;POINT(-99.1332 19.4326)')); copy that hex string into a rust test as bytes; assert the rust decoder produces the same lat/lon I started with. trivial in theory. I am going to write twenty of these before I trust the encoder.

the stakes: this encoder is going to sit underneath every spatial query in the rewritten service. if it has a subtle bug — say, off-by-one on a coordinate, or wrong endianness on a sub-polygon — every distance computation in the system is silently wrong and we don't notice until a driver gets routed through a wall. so I am going to be slow and careful here in a way I am not usually. measure twice, cut once, fuzz a thousand times.

testing plan in more detail:

1. unit tests against psql-emitted known-good EWKB. 20 hand-picked geographies (5 points, 5 linestrings, 5 polygons, 5 multipolygons) covering edge cases (single-point linestring, two-ring polygon, multi-polygon-with-shared-vertex).
2. round-trip tests: rust geography → EWKB → rust decode → assert original geography. 10,000 fuzzed iterations via proptest.
3. integration tests: rust geography → query against actual postgis postgres → SELECT ST_AsText to convert back → assert correct WKT. 50 iterations against the docker-compose postgis instance in CI.
4. property tests: distance computations should be invariant under encoder/decoder round-trip. ST_Distance(rust_encoded, original) == 0 (within float epsilon). 1000 iterations.
5. canary in staging: deploy with the encoder behind a feature flag, dual-read against the python service for 30 days, alert on any spatial-result mismatches >= 1 meter.

that is a lot of testing for a small piece of code. but this small piece of code is going to be the foundation of the whole rewrite, and a subtle bug here would be uniquely catastrophic.

anyway. heads down for the next week on this. updates as I have them. if I am dumb and missing an obvious off-the-shelf solution please tell me, but I have looked hard and there isn't one. this is a real ecosystem gap and I think I just have to do the work.

follow-up brain-dump on the encoder side because I'm too wired to sleep — the test cases I want to cover, in detail:

1. point at (0, 0): the south-pole-of-encoders. EWKB header + 16 zero bytes. simplest possible case.
2. point at (-180, 0): antimeridian crossing, longitude wrapping. should round-trip cleanly.
3. point at (-99.13, 19.43): CDMX. I will be using this constant for the rest of my life as a sanity check.
4. point at (-99.13, 19.43) with explicit SRID 4326 vs implicit: assert that the encoder always emits SRID 4326.
5. linestring with 1 point: degenerate. spec allows it. encoder must handle it.
6. linestring with 47 points: a typical delivery route. realistic shape.
7. linestring with 2000 points: stress test. encoder must not OOM.
8. polygon with one outer ring and zero holes: a simple delivery zone.
9. polygon with one outer ring and three holes: a complex delivery zone (cdmx delivery zones often have university campuses or military bases as holes).
10. polygon where the outer ring is not the first ring in the input: tests our signed-area-based reordering.
11. multipolygon with two disjoint polygons: a tenant covering two cities.
12. multipolygon where one component is a polygon-with-hole: deeply nested encoding.
13. multipolygon with twenty disjoint polygons: stress test for the work-queue.
14. round-trip a polygon with a coordinate that has many decimal places (e.g. -99.1332456789): tests f64 encoding fidelity.

each of these is an expected case + a hex dump from psql + a rust-emitted bytes assertion. I will write the tests this week. that's a day of work. cheap insurance.

Camila brought up the rescue place again. there's still that puppy. one blue eye, one brown. she sent me a photo this afternoon and I might be losing my willpower. we said we'd visit again Saturday.

the puppy in the photo has one blue eye and one brown eye and an ear that flops forward like it gave up halfway through standing up. Camila already named her in the privacy of her own head, I can tell. I have not named her in mine. I am pretending to be the responsible adult here. saturday.

EWKB byte-layout debug session. spent four hours staring at hex dumps of geography points. the issue was endianness — EWKB stores a flag byte for byte-order at position 0, then the type integer in that order. I was reading the type as little-endian regardless of the flag. once I respected the flag byte my decoder started returning correct lat/lon.

I now have a tiny rust crate that round-trips geography(Point, 4326) values to EWKB and back, with hex-tested known-good values from psql's ST_AsEWKB output. compile-time checked queries work for points. tomorrow: linestring.

the four-hour debug was, in retrospect, a textbook case of confirmation bias. I'd convinced myself early that the type integer was always little-endian (because in our environment it always was — we only generated test data on a little-endian machine), and I spent three of the four hours trying to find any other bug while the actual one stared at me from byte 0. the fix, when I finally saw it, was a six-line patch. the lesson: when a binary protocol misbehaves, distrust your assumptions about byte order before you distrust anything else. it's almost always endianness.

the specific debug session went like this: I'd dump the bytes from psql, paste them into a rust test, run my decoder, get a wrong answer, stare at the bytes, run psql again with a different known value, get a wrong answer in a different way, and so on for three hours. the moment of clarity was when I dumped a known geography from a big-endian source (a mac via rosetta — long story) and the byte 0 came back 0x00 instead of 0x01 and my decoder gave a wildly wrong answer and the type integer position was now correctly little-endian but the rest of the bytes were big-endian, and I realized: the flag byte controls the WHOLE rest of the message, and I had hardcoded little-endian in five different read sites.

the fix was to plumb a single 'is_little_endian: bool' state through the decoder and use that flag at every read. six lines added, six places hard-coding LE removed. tests went green. I went to the kitchen and made a fresh round of mate.

one more thing for the day's notes — the proptest setup will catch the next bug like this automatically. round-trip property tests against random EWKB inputs are the right shape of test for binary protocol code. I will be writing more of these as we go. they are cheap to write and they catch a class of bug that no amount of unit testing will. they are the closest thing to formal verification that I'll have access to on this team.

additional debugging note for memory: I found out late that hex-dumping the bytes in groups of (1, 4, 4, 16, 4, etc.) corresponding to EWKB structure makes diffs immediately obvious. naïve hex dumps just look like noise. structured hex dumps tell you exactly which field is wrong. I now have a small rust pretty-printer for EWKB bytes that I use during debugging. five lines of code that have saved me hours.

linestring works. a 47-point delivery route round-trips byte-for-byte through my encoder. carla reviewed the pr, only nit was naming conventions, otherwise she says it looks correct. one chunk down. about 30% of the way to a real PostGIS layer.

47-point delivery route round-trips byte-for-byte. carla says 'looks correct' which from her is approximately a brass-band parade. polygon next. I am going to enjoy this small win for ten minutes before I open the next ticket.

we did it. we have a dog. her name is Nube. blue heeler / Australian Cattle Dog mix, female, 16 weeks old today. born February 14 2024 if the rescue's records are right. she's currently asleep on a folded blanket on the kitchen floor and Camila and I have been taking turns staring at her like she's an alien artifact for the past three hours.

the story. we went back to Hocicos Felices on Saturday morning, telling ourselves we were just going to look. famous last words. she was in the same kennel as last time, except she'd grown half a foot. the volunteer at the rescue, a woman named Sole, said she'd had two adoption interviews and both fell through because the puppy is, in her words, 'a lot.' she nipped one prospective adopter's child on the heel during a meet-and-greet (heeler instincts, no aggression), and she'd chewed through one volunteer's shoelace. red flags for a normal family. perfectly fine for two people who deserve a chaos demon.

Camila knelt down. the puppy walked over and put her chin on Camila's knee. that was the moment. I made a joke about how we shouldn't be making this decision in the first 90 seconds of meeting. nobody listened to me, including me. Sole made gentle eye contact like she'd seen this exact scene fifty times.

the paperwork was straightforward. proof of residence, signed agreement to spay (already scheduled at our vet for month 8), home check waived because Sole said she could read us. donated 2500 MXN beyond the adoption fee. Sole gave us a little bag with the food the rescue had been feeding her, a stuffed lamb that she'd already partially de-stuffed, and a folded pamphlet titled 'so you adopted a heeler' which I will be reading like scripture for the next month.

drove home in la torta with the puppy on Camila's lap, a fresh blanket, a tiny bag of kibble, and zero plan. she was perfectly calm in the car which apparently is rare. she licked Camila's chin twice, watched the city roll past, and then fell asleep with her nose tucked into Camila's elbow. I drove the slowest, smoothest 18 minutes I have ever driven. la torta has never felt more like a luxury sedan.

name. we argued for 90 minutes. Camila wanted Lluvia (rain). I wanted Cometa (comet) which I had never said out loud before but felt right in the moment. we ended up on Nube — cloud — because it covers the blue-heeler coat color (a kind of mottled grey-blue cloud pattern) and because it's short enough to yell during recall training. also Camila said it suited her fluffy little face and I had no good rebuttal.

she is, as predicted, a lot. in the four hours since we got home she has:
- chewed the corner of one Adidas shoe (mine, the older pair, I forgive her)
- discovered the bombilla cup on the low shelf and tried to taste it (I moved it; that cup does not get to be a casualty, abuela would haunt me)
- attempted to herd a roomba
- nipped at Camila's ankles three times when Camila walked too fast across the kitchen (heeler instincts again, we're going to need to learn how to channel this)
- fallen asleep on her side mid-zoom, paws twitching
- woken up 25 minutes later, attacked the stuffed lamb with absolute fury for two minutes, fallen asleep again

the last one is the killer. she just sort of toppled over mid-play and was out for 40 minutes the first time. heeler puppies are apparently like F1 engines — full throttle or off, no idle. the pamphlet says they do this until 6 months when they finally develop the ability to self-regulate. I am taking 'six months' as a number to live with rather than a number to fight against.

plans. we have a vet visit on Tuesday for shots and microchipping. enrolled her in puppy-class at the same place that runs the vet. socialization socialization socialization. heelers without a job become anxious wrecks, and our apartment is 80 sqm — she needs an outlet. I am going to start running with her on the Garmin in like 2 months once her growth plates settle. profe esteban knows a guy who does protection-sports training; I'll see if there's a less-intense version we can do once she's a year old. heelers are bred to make decisions on the move; without that they spiral. we are responsible for giving her a job.

rust migration. the kickoff is happening this week and I just adopted a working-breed puppy. timing is, in a word, lol. but here we are. tech lead by day, frantically googling 'why does my puppy bite ankles' by night. this is the life we have chosen.

she is so small. she fits under my forearm. she has eyebrows that look like she's plotting something. I am writing this with one hand because the other one is on her back. I texted my mom a photo and she replied within 90 seconds saying 'ay mijo qué hermosa, your abuela would have loved her.' I sat with that for a while. I think she would have. abuela liked dogs that herded; she grew up around cattle dogs in Tlaquepaque. she'd have taught Nube to sit by holding a piece of bread above her head and waiting until she figured it out. that's literally how she taught every puppy I remember her interacting with. I bet I could replicate that exact training method from muscle memory because I watched her do it ten times when I was a kid.

so a small loop closes. a heeler puppy in the apartment two years after I started planning to adopt one and seven years after I lost the woman who would have most enjoyed meeting her. life keeps doing this — handing you the right thing on the wrong timing. you take it anyway.

the last thing for tonight. Camila is sitting on the rug with her back against the couch and Nube has crawled into her lap and fallen asleep with her head on Camila's wrist. and Camila is just looking at her. not on her phone. not talking. just looking. and I realized — Camila has been waiting for a small living thing to look at like that since her mother died. she has been holding herself together for nine months and then this puppy walked up to her at a rescue and put her chin on Camila's knee and Camila let go of something. I don't know what to call that something. I don't think you can call it anything. but I saw it and I'm grateful and I'm a little bit broken open about it tonight.

things I want to remember about today specifically:

- Sole at the rescue handing me the pamphlet titled 'so you adopted a heeler' and saying 'okay good luck, when she destroys your apartment remember it's a phase'
- the puppy putting her chin on Camila's knee
- Camila's face when the puppy did that — I watched her decide in real time
- la torta on the way home, slowest 18 minutes, the puppy on Camila's lap with her nose tucked into Camila's elbow
- the moment we agreed on the name (90 minutes of joke names, then both saying 'Nube' at the same time)
- Camila on the rug with the puppy in her lap, just looking at her, not saying anything, for what must have been five full minutes
- the texture of her tiny paws — soft pads with little black spots like ink stains, claws barely past the pad, the whole foot smaller than a peach
- how she folds her ears differently when she's curious vs. when she's scared
- the sound of her sleeping breath, which is somehow louder than I expected for a 5kg animal
- the small triangle of white fur on her chest that the rescue called a 'bib' which I now understand to be a heeler thing
- the absolute dignity with which she approached the empty water bowl and looked at us as if to ask whether we'd noticed our oversight
- the particular smell of puppy breath, which is a chemical I want bottled, which apparently goes away around 6 months and never comes back, which is a piece of information I am going to be processing for the next 18 weeks

what I am quietly worried about, even on this very good night:
- can we do this with the rust migration in full swing
- what happens to her when we travel
- her separation anxiety is going to be real and we have not done the work yet
- she is bonded to Camila harder than to me already, after four hours, and that is going to be a thing

the last one is interesting. heelers attach to one person. literature is consistent on this. it doesn't mean the second person doesn't matter — it means there's a primary and a secondary. Camila is shaping up to be primary. I'll be okay. I am the one who gets walked at 6am while Camila gets snuggled at 2pm. that's a fair trade. I will earn my own kind of bond with her over months and it'll be a different kind, the running-partner kind, the trusts-me-with-the-leash kind. that's enough. it's more than enough.

okay. enough writing. she's about to wake up because heeler puppies have nap cycles like pomodoros. when she wakes up she's going to want food, water, a walk, and a chew toy in that order. I am going to be very glad about all of it.

follow-up at midnight: she has been asleep for ninety minutes uninterrupted and I have been silently in awe of her. she is so small. she is somehow already at home in our apartment. this morning she did not exist in our life and tonight she has a designated water bowl, a designated bed, a sleeping spot near the kitchen vent because she likes the warm air, a chew toy named 'lamb' that she will be sleeping with tonight, a vet appointment next tuesday, a puppy class signed up for, and a name. that is a lot of structure to spin up in twelve hours. a lot of small commitments. each one of them already feels permanent.

I texted my brother Mateo a photo. he responded 'wey ya tienen hijo, felicidades.' which is exactly what he was always going to say, and exactly what I wanted to hear, and which made Camila laugh from the bedroom.

the bombilla cup is now on the high shelf above the spice rack. abuela's cup. saved from a heeler who has not even tried to ascend the spice rack but who, given her track record, will eventually try. the spice rack might also need to come down. dog ownership is, I am realizing, a slow rearrangement of every shelf in the apartment from the bottom up. she occupies the floor, then the couch, then in six months she will jump onto the bed if we let her. she is going to colonize this apartment vertically over the next year and we are going to keep moving things up. small empire.

I am unbelievably tired. I am also unbelievably awake. tomorrow I have a 9am with daniela about the rust phase 1 plan. the rest of my life starts in 8 hours. and somewhere in the middle of all that, this small living animal is going to wake up and need to pee and I am going to be the one to take her downstairs at 5am because Camila has a hard architecture review at 8 and I want her to sleep. small commitments. small commitments. that's the whole game.

Nube ate the corner of the couch. I knew this would happen. she has chosen the corner closest to the window, which has a view, so I respect the strategic thinking. ordered a frozen kong. 36 hours into dog ownership and I have purchased my first piece of dog-specific copium.

the corner of the couch is, to be fair, the corner closest to the window with the best afternoon light, so the puppy chose strategically. I respect the decision. the kong arrives wednesday. between now and then I have ordered an emergency bully stick to bridge the gap. dog ownership is a logistics problem.

polygon support added to the postgis encoder. delivery zones in our schema are stored as MultiPolygon and that's where it gets fiddly because EWKB nests rings inside polygons inside the multi-geom. wrote it recursively, then wrote it iteratively after carla pointed out the recursive version had no depth bound and could stack-overflow on weird inputs. iterative is uglier but safer. fuzzed it against ten thousand random multipolygons generated from psql, no panics. shipping this branch to the rust scaffold today.

performance side: encoder benches at about 1.2 microseconds per route on average geometry. that's negligible compared to the network roundtrip. happy with it.

carla's catch on the recursion is the kind of senior-engineer move that I am keeping in my notes. she didn't say 'this is wrong'; she said 'what happens if the input is 10,000 levels deep, can a malicious or buggy upstream construct that.' the answer was: the rust default stack would overflow before reaching the postgres server, which means a single pathological request could crash a whole pod. the fix was rewriting with an explicit work queue and bounded depth. it took an hour. without that conversation we would have shipped a stack-overflow vector and only found it during a load test or, worse, in prod. the lesson I want to internalize: depth-bound your recursion when the input shape is not under your control. always.

the iterative version uses a Vec as a work-stack, pushing geometry components as it traverses the multipolygon, popping them and writing each one's bytes inline. depth is bounded by the size of the work-stack which we cap at 1024 entries. anything bigger than that returns a clean encode error rather than panicking. we will never see a 1024-deep multipolygon in our actual data; the cap is a paranoia layer.

carla also wrote a fuzz test specifically targeting deep nesting after we shipped this. it uses proptest to generate multipolygons with random nesting up to 2000 deep and asserts that the encoder either succeeds or fails cleanly. ten thousand iterations, zero panics. that is the kind of test you write because of one specific code review comment a senior engineer made, and that test will catch a class of bug for the rest of the service's life. carla earns her senior salary about twice a week.

additional context: I had also added a feature flag wrapping the new encoder so we can fall back to the old bytea-string approach if the encoder misbehaves in production. defense in depth. flags are cheap. surgical rollback is precious. this is the same pattern we'll use for every component of the rewrite — every new thing behind a flag, off by default in prod, on by default in staging. discipline.

on the proptest side: I'm pinning the seed for failures so we can reproduce them. proptest does this by default but it's worth pointing out — when a property fails, the framework saves the seed in proptest-regressions/ and replays it on next run. so a reproducible failing case is automatic. that's the kind of tooling that turns 'flaky test' into 'failing test with a deterministic input,' which is the difference between something you can debug and something you can't.

phase 1 plan, written up in a doc but I'll summarize here for my own benefit.

the two endpoints we're porting first are POST /routes/batch and GET /drivers/{id}/current_assignment. these were the worst offenders in the post-mortem and they're also the highest-traffic. the goal is to get them both serving 1% of production traffic via a feature flag by end of month 9, with the python service still serving the other 99%. dual-write where it matters, dual-read for verification.

for the dual-read verification I'm building a small middleware that, when triggered by a header, calls both python and rust backends, compares the response shapes byte-for-byte (after sorting array fields with non-deterministic ordering), and logs any diff. we'll run that for at least two weeks before we trust the rust path enough to ramp.

data access patterns: /routes/batch is the brutal one with the spatial filter; /drivers/{id}/current_assignment is a pure read with no spatial. start with the easy one for confidence-building.

risk register: PostGIS encoder edge cases we haven't found yet, axum auth-middleware for our custom JWT, Andrés ramping on rust, me being a first-time tech lead. each of those gets a mitigation: 30-day dual-read window for the encoder, carla pair-reviews the auth middleware, twice-weekly pairing sessions for Andrés, weekly tech-lead-coaching from Daniela for me. I am being cautious. the post-mortem reminded me that confidence is what kills you.

the twice-weekly pairing with Andrés is going to be the rate-limiter on phase 1 timeline if I'm not careful. when you pair with a junior, you do less of your own work; when you don't pair with them, they get stuck and you do their work later anyway. there's no version of this that doesn't cost me hours. the right call is to build the pairing in as a non-negotiable and re-baseline timeline assuming the cost.

Daniela's pet phrase is 'the cost of growing engineers is paid in calendar time, not in headcount.' I used to not understand that phrase. I understand it now. you can hire ten more engineers and your timeline doesn't shrink linearly because the senior people have to spend half their time growing the new ones. there is no way out of this trade. you can only choose how openly you account for it.

the risk register I'm keeping has 23 entries currently. each entry has: risk description, likelihood (low/medium/high), impact (low/medium/high), mitigation, owner, status. I review it weekly with carla. it gets shared with daniela monthly. it is not a magic doc. it is a forcing function for me to keep thinking about what could go wrong. doing the thinking is the whole value. the doc itself is a side effect.

the specific tools I'm using to track risk: a markdown table in our team repo, with one row per risk, columns for likelihood/impact/mitigation/owner/status. I keep it short — 23 rows is the max I want to maintain mentally. anything beyond that means we have too many risks and need to reduce by either resolving some or accepting some. the discipline is in pruning the table, not just in growing it. weekly we either resolve a risk or accept it (in which case it leaves the table and lives in 'accepted-risks.md' instead). small ritual. high payoff.

auth middleware done. tower::Layer wrapping a service that decodes our JWT, validates against our auth service (cached for 60s, redis-backed), attaches claims to the request extensions. axum extractor for `Claims` that pulls from extensions. this is the kind of thing that's a 200-line python decorator and a 80-line rust trait+layer impl, but the rust version compiles into something the optimizer can fold flat.

carla reviewed. she pointed out my redis cache key had no namespace and could collide with another service's cache. fixed. small thing but exactly the kind of stuff a senior catches that a junior doesn't.

benchmark: middleware adds 0.4ms p50, 1.2ms p99 to a request. on the python side the same logic was 8ms p50. multiplied across the whole request path the middleware-only savings will probably be 20-30ms. and we haven't even hit the spatial query yet.

the specific way I structured it — and I'm noting this because I want to use the same pattern for future middleware — is: the Layer wraps a Service<Request> -> Future<Response>, the inner code is async, claims-failure short-circuits with a 401, claims-success attaches via request.extensions_mut(). consumers extract via FromRequestParts on a Claims wrapper. it composes cleanly with the trace_layer above it because tower's middleware composition just works as long as you stick to the Service trait. I get tired of hearing 'rust async is hard' from people who haven't actually tried tower. tower is one of the cleaner middleware abstractions I've ever used.

on the redis namespace thing — carla's exact note was 'cache key 'user:123:claims' will collide with the user-service. namespace it: 'routeopt:auth:user:123:claims'.' five-second review comment, hours of future-debugging avoided. the value of senior engineers in code review is comically high and I am going to make sure Andrés sees these reviews so he can internalize the pattern.

the broader point about middleware composition: I am increasingly convinced that tower's middleware model is the cleanest thing rust has produced for web servers. each layer is a Service<Request> -> Future<Response>. you stack them with .layer(). they compose. the type system enforces correctness. the runtime cost is near-zero because the optimizer folds everything together. I will be evangelizing this internally for years.

my axum middleware testing pattern for memory: for each layer write a test that constructs a tower::ServiceBuilder, layers the middleware on, exercises it with synthetic requests, asserts on the response and on side-effects (logs, metrics, cache state). the test is not a full integration test; it's a unit test for the middleware's contract. testing in isolation lets the middleware evolve without breaking the rest of the stack. I will keep doing this for every layer we add.

the broader pattern I'm internalizing: tower's middleware composition is one of the cleaner things I've ever worked with. each Layer wraps a Service and produces a new Service. you stack them with .layer() and the type system enforces correctness. compile-time everything. I cannot overstate how good this is compared to django middleware which is just a list of opaque python classes that are wired up via a settings.py string. type safety end-to-end. love it.

first read endpoint is up. GET /drivers/{id}/current_assignment serving in dev. dual-read against the python service shows 99.7% byte-identical responses. the 0.3% diffs are all in a timestamp-formatting edge case where python is outputting microsecond precision and rust is outputting nanosecond precision. easy fix, going to truncate to microsecond on the rust side to maintain compatibility.

p95 in dev is 11ms vs python's 84ms. that's an 87% reduction. I know dev numbers are not prod numbers but that's a lot of headroom. and the two minutes I spent staring at this latency graph was the best two minutes of my month.

the specific timestamp-precision diff was a kind of revealing one. python's psycopg defaults to microsecond precision because that's what python's datetime supports. rust's chrono::DateTime<Utc> supports nanosecond precision and tokio-postgres preserves it. neither side is wrong; they're just emitting different valid representations of the same moment. but to a downstream client doing string comparison, '2026-04-23T11:42:14.000123Z' and '2026-04-23T11:42:14.000123000Z' are different strings. compatibility-shimming the rust side to truncate-on-output is a one-line fix and the right answer. there will be 30 of these little compatibility shims. I am keeping a doc.

the more general point I keep relearning: a rewrite is not just a port. it is a series of small compatibility shims for behaviors the original system never specified explicitly. you don't realize how many of those you have until you read every diff in a dual-read window. the timestamp-precision diff is one. there will be: trailing-whitespace handling on string fields. null-vs-empty-array handling in arrays. order-of-keys-in-jsonb. case-sensitivity on enum-values. all of them are 'no big deal' until a downstream client breaks because of one.

daniela said something that's stuck with me: 'a port that's faster but slightly different is a port that's broken in subtle ways.' the dual-read is the only thing that keeps us honest. I am planning to leave the dual-read flag on for at least 30 days post-cutover, even after we declare the rust path canonical. the cost is small (one redundant request per shadow request) and the safety is large.

on the broader testing strategy: every endpoint I port gets a 'compatibility contract' test that asserts byte-identical responses against a frozen set of recorded python responses. when we update either side we re-record. this is not perfect — semantic differences that emit the same bytes are missed — but it catches 90% of porting bugs at near-zero cost. I will keep this practice.

the testing pattern that's emerging: every feature gets three tests — a unit test for the function, an integration test for the handler, and a compatibility-contract test against frozen python responses. the third one is the safety net. it catches any subtle behavior change between the python source-of-truth and our rust port. when both pass, we're confident the rewrite is faithful. when only the first two pass, we know we have a behavior diff and we have to decide whether the diff is intended or accidental.

okay it's late and I want to write this down because I've been thinking about it all evening.

four weeks ago I was a senior engineer on a team that was losing to its own architecture. tonight I am a tech lead on a rewrite that's actually shipping, with a dog asleep at my feet, with a dual-read showing 87% latency reduction in dev, with a postgis encoder I wrote myself working in production-ish conditions. four weeks. I feel like I've aged five years and shed five years simultaneously.

the outage was the worst night of my professional career. four hours of staring at pg_stat_activity and feeling personally responsible for 38,000 broken delivery slots. four hours of knowing I'd flagged it and we hadn't moved. I will carry that one for a while.

but the post-mortem became the document that unlocked the rewrite. the rewrite is the thing I have wanted to do for a year. and now I'm doing it. and Daniela is letting me lead it. and the team trusts me enough to follow my pin choices and my crate layout and my risk register. last week Andrés asked me if I had time to look at his draft of the auth tests and I realized — oh, he's asking me because I'm his tech lead now. that's the new shape of the world.

and none of it makes me less anxious. that's the part nobody told me. you get the thing you wanted and the anxiety just changes shape. it's not 'will they let me lead this' anymore, it's 'what if I'm wrong about rust, what if PostGIS bites us in a way I don't see, what if Andrés gets stuck and I can't unstick him because I'm only six months ahead of him in the language, what if the cutover plan I wrote is missing something obvious.' the catalog of possible failures is just longer now.

there's a thing about leading that I am only just understanding. when I was a senior, I owned my own correctness. if I shipped something and it broke, that was on me, in a clean and contained way. as a tech lead, I am responsible for the correctness of code I did not write. Andrés will write a piece of the auth flow next month and I will not have looked at every line of it, and if it breaks production at 3am, it is still my fault — not in the blame sense but in the structural sense, in the sense that it was my job to set up the system so that his code could not break that way. that's a different kind of weight. it's the weight of having to design not just code but the conditions under which other people write code well.

but I am also — I want to say this without sounding cheesy — I am also alive in a way I haven't been since I was 25. there's a thing I wrote tonight (the EWKB iterative encoder for multipolygons) that I am actually proud of in the way I used to be proud of small things. that has not happened to me at work in a long time. I had been moving through code on autopilot for two years. I am not on autopilot now. I sit down at the keyboard and the work pulls me forward. I haven't felt that since university honestly. it is its own drug. I will need to be careful with it; the same fuel that's getting me through the rust migration is also why I'm still typing at 11pm with a dog who needs a walk.

there is an instinct I have to run from this kind of feeling. when things start to feel large I find ways to make them smaller — I disengage, I optimize the wrong corner, I find a small task to hide inside. Camila has called this my 'Russian-doll move.' I open the big problem and inside it there's a smaller problem and I work on the smaller problem and never come back to the big one. I want to not do that with this rewrite. I want to stay in the largeness of it. that's a thing I have to actively choose; it's not my default.

a pattern I notice, sitting with this: every meaningful chapter of the last six months has come from me being forced into something I would not have chosen. the post-mortem because the outage made me write it. the tech lead role because Daniela offered it. the dog because Camila pushed. the conversation with my mom about going to the cemetery because my mom said you said that last month, mijo. left to my own devices I would have continued in the comfortable middle distance from all of this. the things that have made me bigger in the last six months are the things I did not choose. that's a reading I am wary of generalizing because it slides into a kind of fatalism, but it's also empirically true for this stretch of my life. I should think about what it means.

maybe it means I should choose harder things on my own. maybe it means I should tell Camila more often that I want her to push me. maybe it means I should write down, ahead of time, the kinds of pushes I want to be available to. that's a list I have not made. I might make it this weekend.

and the dog is asleep. and Camila is asleep. and I lit a candle for abuela this evening before bed because I wanted to tell her about the rewrite. she would have understood about zero of it. she'd have just said 'qué bien, mijo, que estás haciendo cosas que te gustan, no como esos otros trabajos donde estabas siempre cansado.' which is the most accurate one-line review of my career anyone has ever given me, except she's been dead since 2019 and I'm reciting it from memory.

you know what — maybe that's why I keep talking to her. the version of her in my head is the version of her that gives the cleanest reads on my life. she had no investment in the surface of things. she would have looked at this rewrite and seen, accurately, that I was happy in a way I had not been for years. she would have looked at Nube and seen, accurately, that we were in love with this animal. she would not have asked about the title or the salary. she'd have asked about how I was sleeping and whether I was eating. and the truth is the answers to those questions are mostly: better, mostly. better than last year. eating well. sleeping unevenly but for good reasons.

okay sleep. tomorrow I have to actually decide whether the dual-read window stays at 30 days or whether we can go to 14. carla votes 14, I'm leaning 30. probably split the difference at 21. boring decision-making is the secret content of this whole job. for the moments of glory — the moment the encoder round-tripped, the moment the latency graph dropped — I trade ten thousand small decisions about which window length to use, which retry budget to set, which test to write first. that's the whole gig. I am, at this very specific moment, fine with that.

the other thread I want to pull on tonight: the way the work has been bleeding into how I think about non-work things. I now think about my mom's heart in terms of resilience and graceful degradation. I think about my dad's recovery in terms of mean-time-between-failures. I think about Nube's puppyhood in terms of phased rollouts. that's a kind of contamination that I am uncertain about. on one hand, the engineering frameworks are useful — risk registers and runbooks are not just for software, they help with adult life too. on the other hand, the people in my life are not systems. you can't write a post-mortem for your dad's angina episode and expect his cardiologist to read it.

Camila has been gentle with this. last week she said something like 'you are very good at the kind of love that has a specification.' which was funny and accurate. I love her by completing tasks, by remembering dates, by maintaining the apartment, by managing the dog's vet schedule. those are tasks. she also wants — and rightly — the kind of love that does not have a specification. eye contact in the kitchen. a phone call at lunch with no information transfer. holding her after a hard day at work without trying to debug the day. those are not tasks. the thing I am noticing tonight is that my engineering brain is — paradoxically — also the part of me that is best able to notice this gap. I can write down 'love that does not have a specification' and treat it as a thing to practice. that is itself a specification, which is funny, but also: it works. by writing it down I am more likely to do it. so the specification-brain is in service of the unspecifiable thing rather than at war with it. that's a frame I am going to hold onto.

there's a specific kind of presence I see in Camila that I am trying to learn. when she's with the puppy she is fully with the puppy. she is not also doing six other things. her attention is whole. I have always been a partial-attention person — I am 30% in the room and 70% in three other rooms across the next quarter. she has been, by example, teaching me that attention is the same kind of muscle as biceps. you train it by directing it. you let it atrophy by constantly fragmenting it. she has trained hers; I have fragmented mine. but it's not a fixed trait. I can train mine. it starts with 30 seconds of looking at the dog without thinking about the encoder. that's the rep. I will do the rep more.

one more thing I want on the record before I close the laptop — the thing about being wrong in public, which Daniela said in our 1:1 last month and which I've been chewing on. I had thought she was warning me. she wasn't warning me; she was inviting me. she was saying: this is the price of having ideas in public. the alternative — having ideas only in private — keeps you safer but also keeps you smaller. the rewrite is going to require me to commit publicly to architectural choices that I might be wrong about. when I'm wrong, the public correction is part of the work, not a failure of the work.

spent the day on the spatial query port and I want to capture the whole journey because future me is going to want it.

the target query, in the python service:

SELECT r.id, r.driver_id, r.status, ST_AsGeoJSON(r.path) FROM deliveries_route r INNER JOIN deliveries_stop s ON s.route_id = r.id WHERE r.status IN ('scheduled', 'in_progress') AND r.created_at >= $1 AND ST_DWithin(s.location::geography, $2::geography, 1500) ORDER BY r.created_at DESC LIMIT 100;

in rust the magic part is `center_point as Geography` — that's my custom type going through the encoder I wrote, into EWKB on the wire. `RouteStatus` is a postgres enum that I derived sqlx::Type for. enum-as-text-array binding took me 90 minutes to get right because I had to explicitly cast in the query (`$1::route_status[]`) since sqlx couldn't infer the array type with custom enum elements.

the LATERAL/EXISTS pattern from the python query carries over directly. and crucially the query plan in postgres is the same — index scan with the GIST index on s.location, the EXISTS short-circuits per route. p95 in dev: 23ms vs python's 287ms (that was the post-LATERAL python number). 92% reduction. against the original (pre-LATERAL) python it's a 96% reduction.

two unexpected things: sqlx caches prepared statements per pool by default and our pool was small enough that statement-cache thrash was costing us 1-2ms per request, bumped to 100; and tokio-postgres uses a single multiplexed connection per pool member, so long-running spatial queries can hold a connection blocking shorter queries — bumped pool max to 32 in dev and we'll see.

risk: this query depends on the GIST index being chosen. in production the same planner regression that caused the outage could happen here. mitigation: I'm adding a pg_hint_plan comment as a belt-and-suspenders. I know I griped about hints earlier but in a freshly-rewritten service I want the safety wheels on while we build out the load-test harness. tomorrow: wire up tracing spans so we can see query latencies broken down by handler in datadog.

breaking down where the 23ms goes: 8ms in the spatial index lookup, 11ms in the rest of the planner work and tuple fetch, 3ms in our rust decode + serialize, 1ms in middleware. on the python side the same query was: 240ms in the ORM round-trip (yes, ORM was the dominant cost), 30ms in the index lookup, 17ms in serialize. python wasn't slow because postgres was slow. python was slow because django's ORM did a tremendous amount of work per query. removing django removed the bottleneck.

that finding alone justifies a chunk of this rewrite. the planner regression that caused our outage was real, but the underlying issue was that our application layer was paying enormous overhead per query that masked our actual database utilization. now that the application layer is essentially zero-cost, the database has more headroom to absorb load. the rewrite will probably let us scale on smaller postgres instances than we're currently running, which is a cost win nobody costed into the project.

one more thing I want to write down: I tried the LATERAL+EXISTS pattern again in rust, and the planner picked the same plan it would have picked from python. so the win is not from rewriting the SQL — the win is from removing the python overhead. that's a useful clarification. people often confuse 'rewrite for latency' with 'rewrite the SQL.' in this case the SQL is identical; the application layer is what changed. I'll be careful to communicate that distinction.

the instructor said heelers are notorious for being either great or impossible at puppy class and Nube apparently picked great. she also said 'watch out for adolescence around 8 months' which I am now bracing for.
